title: PRESENTATION V COHOMOLOGY IN THE TOPOS

pages.title: PRESENTATION V COHOMOLOGY IN THE TOPOS

blocks.html:
PRESENTATION V
COHOMOLOGY IN THE TOPOSby J. L. Verdier
Introduction

We present in this presentation the commutative and elementary cohomological invariants topos. In No. 1, we study flat modules and flat morphisms of ringed topos. The demonstrations are made using the hypothesis, most often verified in practice, that the topos have enough points (IV 6). These demonstrations are repeated in the general case in Appendix No. 8 where Deligne, using the technique of local inductive limits, further generalizes in the case of topos, D. Lazard's theorem on the structure of flat modules. The theorems of this exposed, are theorems of the existence of spectral sequences connecting the different cohomological invariants (No. 3, 5, 6). We know that, even for topological spaces, the Čech cohomology does not coincide in general with the cohomology of the sheaves [11]. We introduce in appendix no. 7, a Čech calculation modified allowing to obtain, using overlap, the cohomology of the sheaves in a topos any. In this appendix we are led to use simplicial coverings (hyper-coverings) whose homotopic invariants were studied in [1] (see also [17]).

The cohomological invariants introduced are elementary in the sense that we do not use not the derived categories [12]. The reader familiar with this language will immediately make the translation of the different statements of this presentation and will then be able to generalize them to complexes and hypercohomology. This language of derived categories is also used in the rest of this seminar.

We limit ourselves here to commutative cohomology. For the non-commutative \(H^1\), used in this seminar, and for the non-commutative \(H^2\), we refer to [9]. The functors that we derive are additives; we remain silent on multiplicative structures (cf. [7]).
0. General information on abelian categories
In this issue we recall some lemmas, most of which are found in [11].

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 0.1

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with a generator. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The category \(\mathcal{A}\) satisfies axiom AB 5): Small direct sums are representable and if \((X_i)\), \(i \in I\), is a small growing filter family of sub-objects of an object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(Y\) is a sub-object of \(X\), we have \[ \Bigl(\sup_i X_i\Bigr) \cap Y = \sup_i (X_i \cap Y). \]

  2. ii)

    The small pseudo-filtering inductive limits (I 2.7) are representable and commute at finite projective limits.

In addition, if the above conditions are met, the small filtering inductive limits are universal (I 2.6).

Proof: It is clear that (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). To show that i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii), and to To prove the additional assertion, simply use that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a full subcategory of a category of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on a suitable ring, such as the inclusion functor \(u : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{M}\) admits an exact left adjoint \(v\) [5]. The verification is then trivial.

We know [11] that an abelian category \(\mathcal{A}\) having a generator and verifying axiom AB 5) has sufficient injectives i.e. every object is immersed in an injective object. Moreover, according to the result already cited [5], small products are representable in \(\mathcal{A}\) (axiom AB 3\(^*\))).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 0.2

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) be two abelian categories and \(\mathcal{A} \underset{u}{\overset{v}{\leftrightarrows}} \mathcal{B}\) two adjoint functors (\(u\) is adjoint to the left of \(v\)). Consider both properties:

  1. i)

    The functor \(u\) is exact.

  2. ii)

    The functor \(v\) transforms injective objects of \(\mathcal{B}\) into injective objects of \(\mathcal{A}\).

1) We always have the implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii).

If, moreover, every non-zero object of \(\mathcal{B}\) is the source of a non-zero morphism in an object injective, then (ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (i).

2) Suppose that:

  1. a)

    the category \(\mathcal{B}\) has enough injectives.

  2. b)

    one of the two equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) above is met.

  3. c)

    the functor \(u\) is faithful.

So, the category \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives.
Proof:The proof is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 0.2.1

The category of commutative groups has enough injectives. Applying Lemma 0.2 we deduce that any category of unit modules on a ring with element unit has sufficient injectives. Then applying the result of [5] (used in the proof of 0.1) and 0.2, we deduce that any abelian category having a generator and small inductive limits exact filtering has sufficient injectives; which provides a new demonstration of this done.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 0.3

Let \(\mathcal{A}\), \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathcal{C}\) be three abelian categories and \(u : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\), \(v : \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}\) two left-exact additive functors. Suppose \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) have sufficient injective objects. The following two properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    There exists a spectral functor whose term \(E_2^{p,q}\) is: \[ R^p v R^q u \] and which results in \(R^{p+q} vu\) (suitably filtered).

  2. ii)

    The functor \(u\) transforms injective objects of \(\mathcal{A}\) into acyclic objects for the functor \(v\).
Proof: (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is trivial because it suffices to apply the spectral functor to an injective object. The implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) is proven in [11].

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 0.4

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) be two abelian categories and \(u : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be one left-exact additive functor. Let \(M\) be a subset of the set of objects of \(\mathcal{A}\) having the following properties:

  1. 1)

    Every object of \(\mathcal{A}\) is immersed in an element of \(M\).

  2. 2)

    If \(X \oplus Y\) belongs to \(M\), the object \(X\) belongs to \(M\).

  3. 3)

    If \[ 0 \longrightarrow X' \longrightarrow X \longrightarrow X'' \longrightarrow 0 \] is an exact sequence and if \(X'\) and \(X\) belong to \(M\), then \(X''\) belongs to \(M\) and the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow u(X') \longrightarrow u(X) \longrightarrow u(X'') \longrightarrow 0 \] is correct. Null objects belong to \(M\).

Then every injective belongs to \(M\), and the objects of \(M\) are acyclic for \(u\), i.e. for all \(q \neq 0\) and any object \(X\) of \(M\), we have \(R^q u(X) = 0\). (In particular resolutions by objects of \(M\) allow to calculate the functors derived from \(u\).)
For the proof see [11] 3.3.1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 0.5

Let \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\) be two universes, \(\mathcal{A}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{B}\)) one \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)-category) abelian satisfying axiom AB 5) relative to \(\mathcal{U}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)) and having a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)-small) generating family. Either \(\varepsilon : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an exact and fully faithful functor. The following conditions are equivalents:

  1. 1)

    There exists a generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) such as the family \((\varepsilon(X_i))_{i \in I}\) is generator in \(\mathcal{B}\).

  2. 1')

    Every object of \(\mathcal{B}\) is isomorphic to a quotient of an object of the type \(\bigoplus_{\alpha \in A} \varepsilon(Y_\alpha)\) where \(A \in \mathcal{V}\).

Under these conditions, we have the following property:

  1. 2)

    \(\varepsilon\) transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-small products into products (so commutes to projective limits \(\mathcal{U}\)-small).

In addition, under the equivalent conditions 1) or 1'), the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. a)

    For any object \(Y\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), any subobject of \(\varepsilon(Y)\) is isomorphic like \(\varepsilon\) of a sub-object of \(Y\).

  2. a')

    There exists a generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) such that the family \((\varepsilon(X_i))_{i \in I}\) is generator in \(\mathcal{B}\) and such that for all \(i \in I\), any sub-object of \(\varepsilon(X_i)\) is isomorphic to the image by \(\varepsilon\) of a sub-object of \(X_i\).

  3. b)

    Every object of \(\mathcal{B}\) is isomorphic to a subobject of an object of the type \(\prod_{\alpha \in A} \varepsilon(Y_\alpha)\) where \(A \in \mathcal{V}\).

  4. c)

    \(\varepsilon\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-small direct sums (so commutes at inductive limits \(\mathcal{U}\)-small).

Moreover, under conditions 1) and a') we have:

  1. d)

    \(\varepsilon\) transforms injective objects into injective objects.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 0.5.1

When in 0.5 we have \(\mathcal{U} = \mathcal{V}\), the conditions 1) and a') result in that \(\varepsilon\) is an equivalence of categories (because we then have b), 2) and a) ).

We will limit ourselves to giving indications on the demonstration. The implications 1) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) 1'), 1) \(\Longrightarrow\) 2), are left to the reader. It is clear that a) \(\Longrightarrow\) a'). Let us show that a') entails d). Let \(M\) be an injective in \(\mathcal{A}\). For any \(i \in I\) and any subobject \(Y \hookrightarrow X_i\) of \(X_i\), the homomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(X_i, M) \to \operatorname{Hom}(Y, M)\) is surjective. So, by virtue of a') and the full fidelity of \(\varepsilon\), for any subobject \(U \hookrightarrow \varepsilon(X_i)\), the homomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\varepsilon(X_i), \varepsilon(M)) \to \operatorname{Hom}(U, \varepsilon(M))\) is surjective. As \(\varepsilon(X_i)\) form a generating family of \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\varepsilon(M)\) is injective [11]. Let us show that a') \(\Longrightarrow\) b). Even if it means increasing the family of \(X_i\), we can assume that for all \(i \in I\), any quotient of \(X_i\) is isomorphic to a \(X_j\) for a suitable \(j\). So be it, for everything \(i \in I\), \(X_i \hookrightarrow M_i\) a monomorphism in an injective object. The family \((\varepsilon(X_i))_{i \in I}\) is stable by quotient and for \(i \in I\), the morphism \(\varepsilon(X_i) \hookrightarrow \varepsilon(M_i)\) is, according to d), a monomorphism in an injective object. We then immediately verify that the family \(\varepsilon(X_i)\) being a generator, the \((\varepsilon(M_i))_{i \in I}\) family is a cogenerator, hence b). Let us show that b) \(\Longrightarrow\) c). Let \((Z_\alpha)_{\alpha \in A}\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small family of objects of \(\mathcal{A}\) and let us show that the morphism canonical \[ \bigoplus_{\alpha \in A} \varepsilon(Z_\alpha) \longrightarrow \varepsilon\Bigl(\bigoplus_{\alpha \in A} Z_\alpha\Bigr) \] is an isomorphism. As the family of objects \(\varepsilon(Y)\) is cogenerative, it suffices to show that for any object \(Y\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), the homomorphism \[ \operatorname{Hom}\Bigl(\varepsilon\bigl(\textstyle\bigoplus_\alpha Z_\alpha\bigr), \varepsilon(Y)\Bigr) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}\Bigl(\textstyle\bigoplus_\alpha \varepsilon(Z_\alpha), \varepsilon(Y)\Bigr) \] is an isomorphism which results from the full fidelity of \(\varepsilon\). It remains to show that c) \(\Longrightarrow\) a). Let \(Z\) be a sub-object of \(\varepsilon(Y)\). There exists, by virtue of 1'), a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small family \(Y_\alpha\) of objects from \(\mathcal{A}\) and an epimorphism from \(\bigoplus \varepsilon(Y_\alpha)\) to \(Z\). As \(\varepsilon\) switches to direct \(\mathcal{U}\)-small sums, there therefore exists an epimorphism \(\varepsilon(Y') \to Z\). Let us note \(u : \varepsilon(Y') \to Z \to \varepsilon(Y)\) the compound morphism. We have \(Z = \operatorname{Im}(u)\). Like \(\varepsilon\) is fully faithful, we have \(u = \varepsilon(v)\) and consequently \(Z = \operatorname{Im}(\varepsilon(v)) = \varepsilon(\operatorname{Im}(v))\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 0.5.2.

Let \(\operatorname{Sex}_{\mathcal{V}}(\mathcal{A})\) be the category of contravariant functors of \(\mathcal{A}\) in the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-commutative groups which commute at inductive limits \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. Show that under conditions 1) and a') the canonical functor of \(\mathcal{B}\) in \(\operatorname{Sex}_{\mathcal{V}}(\mathcal{A})\) is an equivalence.
1. Flat modules

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos (IV 11.1.1). A \(A\)-Module on the right (resp. on the left) \(M\) is said to be flat if the functor \(M \otimes_A -\) (resp. \(- \otimes_A M\)) of the category of \(A\)-Modules on the left (resp. right) in the category of abelian bundles of \(E\), is correct.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2

Let \(M\) be a \(B\text{-}A\) bi-Module.

  1. 1)

    The following properties are equivalent:

    1. i)

      Module \(M\) is \(A\)-flat on the left.

    2. ii)

      For any \(B\)-Injective module \(I\), the \(A\)-Module on the left \(\mathscr{H}om_B(M, I)\) is injective.

  2. 2)

    A Module \(M\), pseudo-filtering inductive limit (I 2.7.1) of Flat Modules, is flat.

  3. 3)

    Finally, if \(M_\bullet = \dots M_{i+1} \to M_i \dots\) is an acyclic complex of modules dishes (\(M_i = 0\) for \(i < i_0\)), then for any Module \(F\), the complex: \[ M_\bullet \otimes_A F = \dots M_{i+1} \otimes_A F \longrightarrow M_i \otimes_A F \dots \] is acyclic.

Proof: According to (IV 12.12) we have an addition isomorphism: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_B(M \otimes_A -, -) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_A(-, \mathscr{H}om_B(M, -)) . \] It is then sufficient to apply 0.2 to obtain the equivalence (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii). This isomorphism addition also shows that the tensor product commutes at inductive limits. The fact that the pseudo-filtering inductive limits are exact (0.1) results in the second assertion. To show that the complex \(M_\bullet \otimes_A F\) is acyclic, it suffices to show that for any injective abelian beam \(I\) the complex \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(M_\bullet \otimes_A F, I)\) is acyclic. This complex is isomorphic, in by virtue of the addition formulas, to the complex \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(F, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(M_\bullet, I))\). But according to the equivalence (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii), the complex of sheaves \[ \mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(M_\bullet, I) \] is an acyclic complex whose objects are injective, hence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3.1

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(H\) be an object of \(E\), \(M\) be \(A_{/H}\)-Flat module. Then \(j_{H!} M\) is a \(A\)-flat module. In particular \(A_H\) is flat on the right and left.

Suppose, to clarify ideas, that \(M\) is a \(A_{/H}\)-Module on the right. For everything \(A\)-Module on the left \(P\), we have a canonical isomorphism (IV 12) \[ P \otimes_A j_{H!} M \simeq j_{H!}(P \otimes_{A_{/H}} M) . \] The functors \(j_{H!}\) and \(j_H^*\) are exact (IV 11.3.1 and 11.12.2) and by hypothesis, the functor \(- \otimes_{A_{/H}} M\) is correct. Consequently the functor \(P \longrightarrow P \otimes_A j_{H!} M\) is exact and \(j_{H!} M\) is flat.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3.2 (Projection formula for closed immersions)

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(i : F \to E\) be a closed subtopos of \(E\). Let's set \(i^*A = A_{/F}\). For all \(A_{/F}\)-Module (right) \(M\) and all \(A\)-Module (left) \(P\), we have a functorial isomorphism \[ i_*(M \otimes_{A_{/F}} i^*P) \simeq (i_*M) \otimes_A P . \]

Let \(U\) be the open complement of \(F\) and \(j : U \to E\) be the canonical immersion morphism. We have \(j^*(i_*M \otimes_A P) \simeq 0 \otimes_{A_{/U}} j^*(P)\) (IV 12); as a result \(i_*M \otimes_A P\) has its support in \(F\). So the addition morphism \(i_*M \otimes_A P \to i_*i^*(i_*M \otimes_A P)\) is an isomorphism (IV 14). We have \(i^*(i_*M \otimes_A P) \simeq i^*i_*M \otimes_{A_{/F}} i^*P\) (IV 12) and since \(i : F \to E\) is a closed immersion, \(i^*i_*M \simeq M\) (IV 14); hence the isomorphism announced.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.3

For any \(A_{/F}\)-Flat module \(M\), \(i_*M\) is flat.
It results from 1.3.2. and from (IV 14) that the functor \(P \longmapsto (i_*M) \otimes_A P\) is a functor correct.

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(x : P \to E\) be a point of \(E\) (IV 6.1), \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\) the category of neighborhoods of \(x\) (IV 6.8). Every object \(V\) of \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\) corresponds to an object of \(E\), again noted \(V\), and a point \(x_V : P \to E/V\) of \(E/V\). Moreover, at any morphism \(u : V \to W\) of \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\), corresponds to an essentially commutative diagram of topos morphisms (IV 6.7)
(1.4.1) \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^{x_V} \ar[dr]_{x_W} & E/V \ar[d]^{j_u} \\ & E/W } \]
Let \(N\) be a \(A_x\)-module (resp. a set). From the diagram (1.4.1), we deduce a diagram of \(A_x\)-modules (resp. sets):
(1.4.2) \[ \xymatrix{ x_W^* x_{W*}N \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{ad}_W} \ar[d]_{\phi(u)} & N \\ x_V^* x_{V*}N \ar[ur]_{\mathrm{ad}_V} & } \]

where \(\mathrm{ad}_W\) and \(\mathrm{ad}_V\) are the addition morphisms. In addition, we immediately check that \(\phi(uv) = \phi(v)\,\phi(u)\). We therefore have a functor of the filtering category \(\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ\) in the category of \(A_x\)-modules (resp. sets) and a homomorphism:
(1.4.3) \[ \Lambda(N) : \varinjlim_{V \in \mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} x_V^* x_{V*}N \longrightarrow N \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.5

For all \(A_x\)-modules (resp. set) \(N\), \(\Lambda(N)\) is a isomorphism.

This proposition is a special case of a proposition due to Deligne (8.2.6). Let's give some a direct demonstration. By passing to the underlying sets it suffices to prove the proposition when \(N\) is a set (I 2.8). The cofilter category \(\mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x))\) of the arrows of \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\) is fiberized on the category \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\) by the functor \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x)) \to \mathrm{vois}(x)\) which, with an arrow, associates its goal. Let \(D\) be a category and \(F : \mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x)) \to D\) be a pro-object (I 8.10). For any object \(V\) of \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\), let us denote \(F_V\) the pro-object obtained by restricting the functor \(F\) to fiber category \(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V\). At any morphism \(m : U \to V\) of \(\mathrm{vois}(x)\), the functor base change by \(m\) associates a morphism of the pro-object \(F_U\) into the pro-object \(F_V\) and the canonical pro-object morphisms \(F \to F_V\) determine a pro-object morphism:
(1.5.1) \[ F \longrightarrow \varinjlim_{V \in \mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} F_V \]
which we immediately verify is an isomorphism. Let us apply this remark to the pro-object of pointed sets:
(1.5.2) \[ (U, V, m) \longmapsto x_V^*(m) = F(U, V, m) . \]
We obtain an isomorphism of pro-objects:
(1.5.3) \[ F \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} \varinjlim_{(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V)^\circ} x_V^*(m) ; \]
hence, for any set \(N\), a bijection

(1.5.4) \[ \varinjlim_{\mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x))^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(m), N) \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} \varinjlim_{(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(m), N) . \]

But, for \(V\) fixed, \(\varinjlim_{(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(m), N)\) identifies with \(x_V^* x_{V*}N\). Indeed, we have \(x_V^* x_{V*}N \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x_V)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}_{E/V}(W, x_{V*}N)\) according to IV 6.8.1 therefore, by addition, we have \(x_V^* x_{V*}N \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x_V)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(w), N)\) and more the category \(\mathrm{vois}(x_V)\) is equivalent to the category \(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V\) (IV 6.7.2). Finally, the applications transitional canons \[ \varinjlim_{(\mathrm{vois}(x)/U)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_U^*(m), N) \longrightarrow \varinjlim_{(\mathrm{vois}(x)/V)^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(n), N) \] in 1.5.4 identify with canonical applications \(x_U^* x_{U*}N \longrightarrow x_V^* x_{V*}N\) (1.4.3) as well that the reader would like to verify. We therefore have a bijection
(1.5.5) \[ \varinjlim_{\mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x))^\circ} \operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(m), N) \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} x_V^* x_{V*}N \]

and the application \(\Lambda(N) : \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} x_V^* x_{V*}N \longrightarrow N\) (1.4.3) composed with bijection 1.5.5 comes from applications \(\operatorname{Hom}(x_V^*(m), N) \longrightarrow N\) which has an application \(r : x_V^*(m) \longrightarrow N\) associates the image with \(r\) of the point marked with \(x_V^*(m)\). To demonstrate the proposition, it is then sufficient to notice that any object \((U, V, m)\) of \(\mathrm{Fl}(\mathrm{vois}(x))\) is reduced by \((U, U, \mathrm{id}_U)\) and \(x_U^*(\mathrm{id}_U)\) is reduced to one element or, in other words, that the morphism canonical constant pro-object reduced to one element in \(F\) is an isomorphism of pro-objects.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.6

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module.

  1. 1)

    When \(M\) is flat, for any point \(x : P \to E\) of \(E\), the \(A_x\)-module \(M_x\) is flat.

  2. 2)

    Let \((x_i)_{i \in I}\) be a conservative point family of \(E\) such that for any \(i \in I\), \(M_{x_i}\) is a \(A_{x_i}\)-flat module.

Then \(M\) is flat.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.1

Let \(M\) be a Flat Module and \(V\) be an object of \(E\). The \(A_{/V}\)-Module \(j_V^* M\) is flat.

We must show that the functor \(P \longmapsto P \otimes_{A_{/V}} j_V^* M\) is exact. Like the functor extension by zero \(j_{V!}\) is exact and faithful (IV 11.3.1), it suffices to show that the functor \(P \longmapsto j_{V!}(P \otimes_{A_{/V}} j_V^* M)\) is correct. We have a functorial isomorphism \(j_{V!}(P \otimes_{A_{/V}} j_V^* M) \simeq j_{V!}(P) \otimes_A M\) (IV 12.11) and consequently the functor \(P \longmapsto j_{V!}(P) \otimes_A M\) is correct (IV 11.3.1).

Let's demonstrate 1). Let us suppose to fix the ideas that \(M\) is a \(A\)-Flat left module and let \(x : P \to E\) be a point of \(E\). Let us show that the functor \(N \longmapsto N \otimes_{A_x} M_x\) of the category of \(A_x\)-modules on the right in the category of commutative groups is correct. It is enough for this shows that this functor is exact on the left. With notations of 1.4, let \(V\) be a neighborhood of \(x\) and \(j_V : E/V \to E\) the location morphism. We have, for all \(A_x\)-module \(N\), \[ x_V^* x_{V*}N \otimes_{A_x} M_x \simeq x_V^* x_{V*}N \otimes_{A_x} x_V^*(j_V^* M) \simeq x_V^*(x_{V*}N \otimes_{A_{/V}} j_V^* M) \] (IV 12.11). So the functor \(N \longmapsto x_V^* x_{V*}N \otimes_{A_x} M_x\) is left exact. Subsequently, the functor \(N \longmapsto N \otimes_{A_x} M_x\), filtering inductive limit of left-exact functors (1.5), is exact left. Let us demonstrate 2). Either \[ 0 \longrightarrow P' \longrightarrow P \longrightarrow P'' \longrightarrow 0 \] an exact sequence of \(A\)-Modules and show that the sequence \(0 \to P' \otimes_A M \to P \otimes_A M \to P'' \otimes_A M \to 0\) is correct. To do this, it is enough to show that for all \(i \in I\), the sequence obtained by passing to the fibers in \(x_i\) is exact (IV 6). But the continuation of fibers in \(x_i\) is the continuation (IV 13.5) \[ 0 \longrightarrow P'_{x_i} \otimes_{A_{x_i}} M_{x_i} \longrightarrow P_{x_i} \otimes_{A_{x_i}} M_{x_i} \longrightarrow P''_{x_i} \otimes_{A_{x_i}} M_{x_i} \longrightarrow 0 \] and since \(M_{x_i}\) is flat, this sequence is exact.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.7

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(u : E' \to E\) be a morphism of topos and poses \(A' = u^*A\). Let \(M\) be a \(A'\)-Module on the right (resp. on the left). The following conditions are equivalents:

  1. i)

    The functor \(N \longmapsto M \otimes_{A'} u^*N\) (resp. \(N \longmapsto u^*N \otimes_{A'} M\)) of the category of \(A\)-Modules on the left (resp. on the right) in the category of abelian bundles of \(E'\) is correct.

  2. ii)

    \(M\) is a \(A'\)-Flat module.

It is clear that ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). Let us show that i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). We will not demonstrate only in the case where \(E'\) has a conservative family of points \((x_i)_{i \in I}\). The general case is treated in (8.2.7). In this particular case, which covers most applications, we are brought back to the case where \(E'\) is the punctual topos and where, consequently, the morphism \(u\), which we will note \(x\), is a point of \(E\) (1.6. and IV 11.3.1). We will limit ourselves to the case where \(M\) is a \(A'\)-Module on the right. The case where \(M\) is a \(A'\)-Module on the left returns there by passing to the opposite rings. Let \(V\) be a neighborhood of \(x\) and \(V_x\) its fiber in \(x\). We have, with the notations of 1.4, an essentially commutative diagram of topos morphisms:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ P \ar[r]^{j} \ar[dr]_{x_V} & P/V_x \ar[r]^{j_{V_x}} \ar[d]^{x/V} & P \ar[d]^{x} \\ & E/V \ar[r] & E } \]

where \(x/V\) is the morphism deduced from \(x\) by localization on \(V\) (IV 5.10) and \(j\) is the morphism deduced from the point marked with \(V_x\). Let us show that the functor \(N' \longrightarrow M \otimes_{A'} x_V^*N'\) is exact. We have \(x_V^* = j^*(x/V)^*\) and \(\mathrm{id} = j^* j_V^*\) and as a result, we have a canonical isomorphism \(M \otimes_{A'} x_V^*N' \simeq j^*(j_{V_x}^* M \otimes_{A'_{/V}} (x/V)^*N')\). As the functor \(j^*\) is exact and as the functor \(j_{V_x!}\) is exact and faithful (IV 11.3.1), it suffices to show that the functor \(N' \longmapsto j_{V_x!}(j_{V_x}^* M \otimes (x/V)^*N')\) is exact, and therefore (IV 12.11), it suffices to show that the functor \(N' \longmapsto M \otimes_{A'} j_{V_x!}(x/V)^*N'\) is exact. But it results from the definition of the inverse image of an induced topos (IV 5.10.2) that \(j_{V_x!}(x/V)^*N'\) is equal to \(x^* j_{V!}N'\) and like the functor \(j_{V!}\) is exact (IV 11.3.1), the functor \(N' \longmapsto M \otimes_{A'} x^* j_{V!}N'\) is exact by hypothesis. For all \(A'\)-module \(Q\), we have a functorial isomorphism (1.5): \[ Q \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varinjlim_{\mathrm{vois}(x)^\circ} x_V^* x_{V*}Q . \] According to the above, the functor \(Q \longmapsto M \otimes_{A'} Q\) is filtering inductive limit of functors exact on the left. It is therefore exact on the left and therefore \(M\) is flat.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.7.1

Let \(u : (E', A') \to (E, A)\) be a morphism of ringed topos, \(M\) a \(A\)-Flat module. Then \(u^*M\) is a \(A'\)-Flat module.
Results from the criterion given in 1.7 and the formula (IV 13.4.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.7.2

Let \(u : (E', A') \to (E, A)\) be a morphism of ringed topos. The conditions following are equivalent:

  1. i)

    the \(u^{-1}(A)\)-Module on the right (resp. left) \(A'\) is flat.

  2. ii)

    the functor \(u^*\) of the category of \(A\)-Modules on the left (resp. on the right) in the category of \(A'\)-Module on the left (resp. on the right) is correct.
The equivalence results from 1.7 and the definition of \(u^*\) (IV 13.2.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.8

A ringed topos morphism that has the equivalent properties of 1.7.2 is called a flat left topos morphism (resp. right).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.9

Let \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\) be two universes, \(C\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) and \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the categories of \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles of sets respectively, \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the functor canonical inclusion, \(A\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ring bundle on \(C\).

  1. 1)

    The functor \(\varepsilon\) commutes at inductive and projective limits \(\mathcal{U}\) - Small Modules. The functor \(\varepsilon\) is conservative and fully faithful on the categories of Modules.

  2. 2)

    For any object \(H\) of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), there exists an isomorphism canonical \(\varepsilon(A_H) \simeq \varepsilon(A)_{\varepsilon(H)}\).

  3. 3)

    Any \(A\)-Injective module on the left or right is transformed by \(\varepsilon\) in \(\varepsilon(A)\)-Injective module.

  4. 4)

    Any \(A\)-Flat module on the right or left is transformed by \(\varepsilon\) into \(\varepsilon(A)\)-Flat module.

  5. 5)

    The functor \(\varepsilon\) commutes to form the tensor product and the bundle of homomorphisms.

The formation of the associated beam does not depend on the universe (II 3.6). It then results from the construction of inductive and projective limits in the categories of bundles (II 4.1 and 6.4) that the functor \(\varepsilon\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-small projective limits of sets or Modules, hence 1). Let us demonstrate 2). By taking a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small generating subcategory of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), we can be reduced to the case where \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small (III 4.1). It then follows from (II 6.5) that the \(A\)-Free module generated by \(H\) is obtained by forming the pre-bundle of \(A\)-free modules generated by \(H\), formation which switches to the enlargement of the universes, then by forming the associated beam, an operation which also switches to the enlargement of the universes (II 3.6). To prove 3) it is enough to show, by virtue of 0.5, that any sub-\(\mathcal{V}\)-bundle of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle which is quite clear. Let us demonstrate 4). Let \(M\) be a \(A\)-Flat module. It suffices to show that for any \(\varepsilon(A)\)-Injective module \(J\), the abelian beam \(\mathscr{H}om_{\varepsilon(A)}(\varepsilon(M), J)\) is injective (1.2). Under 0.5, \(J\) is sub-object, therefore direct factor of an object of the type \(\prod_\alpha \varepsilon(I_\alpha)\), where the \(I_\alpha\) are injectives. We can therefore assume that \(J = \varepsilon(I)\) where \(I\) is an injective object. If the homomorphism \(\varepsilon(\mathscr{H}om_A(M, I)) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_{\varepsilon A}(\varepsilon M, \varepsilon(I))\) is a isomorphism we deduce that \(\mathscr{H}om_{\varepsilon(A)}(\varepsilon(M), \varepsilon(I))\) is injective according to 3). It therefore remains to be demonstrated 5). The fact that the formation of the tensor product commutes to the functor \(\varepsilon\) results from IV 12.6 and from the fact that the formation of the associated beam switches to the enlargement of the universe (II 3.6). For any object \(X\) of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) and any pair of \(A\)-Modules \(M\) and \(N\) of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) we therefore have \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(\varepsilon X, \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}}(X, \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_A(\mathbf{Z}_X \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} M, N) \] by virtue of the full fidelity of \(\varepsilon\), then \[ \operatorname{Hom}_A(\mathbf{Z}_X \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} M, N) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\varepsilon A}(\varepsilon(\mathbf{Z}_X \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} M), \varepsilon N) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\varepsilon A}(\varepsilon\mathbf{Z}_X \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} \varepsilon M, \varepsilon N) \] by virtue of the full faithfulness of \(\varepsilon\) and the foregoing. Then using 2) and IV 12.14, we obtain ultimately an isomorphism \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(\varepsilon X, \varepsilon\mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(\varepsilon X, \mathscr{H}om_{\varepsilon A}(\varepsilon M, \varepsilon N)) ; \] hence the isomorphism announced.

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) be a small family of morphisms with the same goal. For any finite set \(\Delta_n = [0, \dots, n]\), let us set \[ S_n(\mathcal{U}) = \coprod_{f : \Delta_n \to I} U_{f(1)} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(n)} , \] the sum being taken over all the applications of \(\Delta_n\) in \(I\). Let \(m\) and \(n\) be two integers, \(g : \Delta_m \to \Delta_n\) an application. We define a morphism
(1.10.1) \[ s(g) : S_n(\mathcal{U}) \to S_m(\mathcal{U}) , \]

as follows: for all \(f : \Delta_n \to I\), the restriction of \(s(g)\) to \(U_{f(1)} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(n)}\) is the compound morphism of the canonical inclusion \[ U_{f(g(1))} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(g(m))} \longrightarrow S_m(\mathcal{U}) , \] and the unique morphism \[ s_f(g) : U_{f(1)} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(n)} \longrightarrow U_{f(g(1))} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(g(m))} \] such as for all \(i \in \Delta_m\) \[ \operatorname{pr}_{f(g(i))} s_f(g) = \operatorname{pr}_{f(gi)} \] (\(\operatorname{pr}_\alpha\) designates the \(\alpha\)-th projection). We thus obtain a contravariant functor \(\Delta_n \longmapsto S_n\) from the category of finite sets in \(E\); in other words a complex semi-simplicial \(S_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) of objects from \(E\). Note that this complex is canonically increased to \(X\). Any functor of \(E\) in a category \(C\) transforms \(S_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) into a simplicial object by \(C\). In particular if \(A\) is a Ring of \(E\), the functor "\(A\)-Generated free module" transforms \(S_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) into a simplicial complex of \(A\)-biModules increased towards \(A_X\) and noted \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\). We have
(1.10.2) \[ A_n(\mathcal{U}) = \bigoplus_{f : \Delta_n \to I} A_{U_{f(1)} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{f(n)}} . \]
This complex will often be noted

(1.10.3) \[ \xymatrix@C=3.5em{ \dots \ar@<1ex>[r]^{s_0} \ar[r]|{s_1} \ar@<-1ex>[r]_{s_2} & \bigoplus_{i,j} A_{U_i \times_X U_j} \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{s_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{s_1} & \bigoplus_i A_{U_i} \ar[r] & A_X } \]

where the arrows of 1.10.3 (except the last which is the increase) represent the face operators of simplicial complex \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\), that is to say the morphisms corresponding to the applications increasing injectives of \(\Delta_n\) in \(\Delta_{n+1}\) (\(s_i\) avoids the integer \(i\)). At the resort \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\), we associate a differential complex increased towards \(A_X\):
(1.10.4) \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \dots \ar[r]^-{d} & \bigoplus_{i,j} A_{U_i \times_X U_j} \ar[r]^-{d} & \bigoplus_i A_{U_i} \ar[r] & A_X } \]
by posing

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.11

When the family \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) is epimorphic, the differential complex 1.10.4 is acyclic and provides a resolution of \(A_X\).

Let us note \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}\) the constant bundle of values ​​\(\mathbf{Z}\). By definition of the functor "\(A\)-Generated free module", we have, for any object \(H\) of \(E\), \[ A_H \simeq \underline{\mathbf{Z}}_H \otimes_{\underline{\mathbf{Z}}} A , \] hence \[ A_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \simeq \underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \otimes_{\underline{\mathbf{Z}}} A . \] As the components of \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) are flat \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}\)-Modules ((1.3.1)), just show the proposition when \(A = \underline{\mathbf{Z}}\).

Let us first assume that \(E\) is the topos of the sets. Then the increased complex \(S_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) is a direct sum of augmented complexes of the type \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \dots \ar@<1.5ex>[r] \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-1.5ex>[r] & S \times S \times S \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & S \times S \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & S \ar[r] & \{e\} } \] (\(\{e\}\) one-element set). Each of these augmented complexes is homotopically trivial. So \(S_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) is a homotopically trivial augmented complex and therefore its homology is trivial hence the proposition in this case.

Let \(p : \mathrm{Ens} \to E\) now be a point of \(E\). Like the formation of the complex \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) commutes to inverse image functors by topos morphisms, \(p^*(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U})) \simeq \underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(p^*(\mathcal{U}))\) is a resolution from \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{p^*X} \simeq p^*(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_X)\); hence the proposition when \(E\) has sufficient fiber functors (IV 4.6). This is the case in particular when \(E\) is a topos of pre-sheaves \(\widehat{C}\) because for any object \(X\) of \(C\), \(\Gamma(X, -)\) is a fiber functor. In the case general, \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of bundles on a small site \(C\) (IV 1) and the epimorphic family \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) is the image, by the "associated sheaf" functor, of an epimorphic family \(\mathcal{U}' = (U'_i \to X')_{i \in I}\). Therefore \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \simeq a\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_\bullet(\mathcal{U}')\) is a resolution of \(a\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{X'} \simeq \underline{\mathbf{Z}}_X\).
2. Čech cohomology. Cohomological notation
2.1 General rating

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M, N\) two \(A\)-Modules (on the left to fix the ideas). We note \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; M, N)\) the value in \(N\) of the \(q\)-th functor right derivative of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(M, \cdot)\) [11]. The functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; M, N)\) \(q \in \mathbf{N}\), form a \(\delta\)-functor into variable \(N\). It is also a \(\delta\)-functor contravariant in the variable \(M\). We have, by definition, \(\operatorname{Ext}^0_A(E; M, N) = \operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N)\).

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\). When \(M = A_X\), the \(A\)-Free module generated by \(X\) (IV 12), we set \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; A_X, N) = H^q(X, N)\). It will be noted that in this new notation, the ring \(A\) is no longer present. This cannot lead to confusion because we will show (3.5) that the formation of \(H^q(X, \cdot)\) switch to scalar restriction. The functor \(H^q(X, \cdot)\) is the \(q\)-th functor right derivative of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(A_X, \cdot) = \operatorname{Hom}_E(X, \cdot)\) also denoted \(\Gamma(X, \cdot)\). When \(M = A\), we set \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; A, N) = H^q(E, N)\).
2.2 Location.

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \(j : E_{/X} \to E\) the location morphism (IV 8). The functor \(j_X^*\) for Modules, is exact (4.11) and admits a left adjoint functor \(j_{X!}\) exact (4.11). By then it transforms the injective Modules into injective Modules. We therefore have, for a \(A\)-Module variable \(N\) of \(E\) and a \(A|X\)-Module \(M\) variable of \(E_{/X}\), functorial isomorphisms
(2.2.1) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A|X}(E_{/X}; M, j_X^* N) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; j_{X!}M, N) . \]
In particular, we have canonical isomorphisms \[ H^q(E_{/X}, j_X^* N) \simeq H^q(X, N) . \] For any object \(X\) of \(E\) and any pair \(M\) and \(N\) of \(A\)-Modules, we set:
(2.2.2) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M, N) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A|X}(E_{/X}; M|X, N|X) . \]

From the above, the functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M, \cdot)\) are the functors derived from functors \(N \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom}_{A|X}(M|X, N|X)\). The functor \((M, N) \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M, N)\), \(q > 0\), form a \(\delta\)-functor with respect to each of the variables.
2.3 Case of pre-beam topos. Cohomology of an overlap.

Let \(C\) be a small category provided with a pre-bundle of rings \(A\), \(\widehat{C}\) the topos of pre-beams on \(C\), \(X\) a representable object on \(\widehat{C}\). The functor that associates with a \(A\)-Module \(M\) the group \(\Gamma(X, M) = M(X)\) is exact (I 3). Consequently \(H^q(X, M) = 0\) for all \(q > 0\) and all \(A\)-Module \(M\) or that \(A_X\) is a \(A\)-Projective module.

Let \(S\) be a pre-beam on \(C\). We have a canonical isomorphism for all \(A\)-Module \(M\) (I 2): \[ \Gamma(S, M) \simeq \varprojlim_{C/S} M|S \] Additionally, for any injective \(A\)-Module \(M\), the \(A|S\)-Module \(M|S\) is injective (2.2). Subsequently, the groups \(H^q(S, M)\) are the values in \(M|S\) of the functors derived to the right of the functor \(\varprojlim_{C/S}\). In noting \(\varprojlim^q_{C/S}\) these derived functors, we have canonical isomorphisms: \[ H^q(S, M) \overset{\sim}{\to} \varprojlim{}^q_{C/S} M|S . \] In particular, we have canonical isomorphisms \[ H^q(\widehat{C}, M) \overset{\sim}{\to} \varprojlim{}^q_{C} M . \]

Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) and \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family of morphisms of \(C\) such that for all \(i \in I\), \(U_i \to X\) is quarable (I 10). Let us note \(A_\bullet\) the complex simplicial studied in 1.10: \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ A_\bullet = \dots \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & \coprod_{(i,j) \in I \times I} A_{U_i \times_X U_j} \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \coprod_{i \in I} A_{U_i} . } \] For all \(A\)-Module \(M\), we set \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, M) = \operatorname{Hom}_A(A_\bullet, M)\):

(2.3.3.1) \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, M) : \prod_{i \in I} M(U_i) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \prod_{(i,j) \in I \times I} M(U_i \times_X U_j) \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & \dots . } \]
We set \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, M) = H^q(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, M))\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3.4
  1. 1)

    With the notations of 2.3.3, let \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be the sieve generated by the family \((U_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\). We have a canonical isomorphism \[ H^q(\mathcal{U}, M) \simeq H^q(R, M) . \]

  2. 2)

    Functors \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, \cdot)\) commute to scalar restrictions.

As \(R\) is a subobject of \(X\) in \(\widehat{C}\), fiber products \(U_{i_1} \times_R U_{i_2} \times_R \dots \times_R U_{i_n}\) and \(U_{i_1} \times_X U_{i_2} \times_X \dots \times_X U_{i_n}\) are canonically isomorphic. It follows from 1.11 that the complex \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) is a resolution of \(A_R\) and 2.3.1 that the components of \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) are Projective Modules. The groups of cohomology of \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, M)\) are therefore canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(\widehat{C}; A_R, M)\), hence the isomorphism. Assertion 2) follows immediately from the description of \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, M)\) (2.3.3.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.5

Let \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X, i \in I)\) and \(\mathcal{U}' = (U'_j \to X, j \in J)\) two families of goal morphisms \(X\); Let \(\varphi = (\varphi : I \to J, m_i : U_i \to U'_{\varphi(i)})\) and \(\varphi' = (\varphi' : I \to J, m'_i : U_i \to U'_{\varphi'(i)})\) two morphisms (above \(X\)) of \(\mathcal{U}\) in \(\mathcal{U}'\). The morphisms \(\varphi\) and \(\varphi'\) induce the morphisms \(\varphi^q\) and \(\varphi'^q\): \(\varphi'^q : H^q(\mathcal{U}', M) \longrightarrow H^q(\mathcal{U}, M)\). The morphisms \(\varphi^q\) and \(\varphi'^q\) are equal. In particular if the families \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathcal{U}'\) are equivalent (i.e. if it exists a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\) in \(\mathcal{U}'\) and a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}'\) in \(\mathcal{U}\)) the \(A\) - Modules \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, M)\) and \(H^q(\mathcal{U}', M)\) are canonically isomorphic.

Proof: Indeed, let \(R\) and \(R'\) be the sieves of \(X\) generated by the families \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathcal{U}'\). The morphisms \(\varphi\) and \(\varphi'\) define the same morphism of \(R\) in \(R'\) and therefore induce two homotopic morphisms between the projective resolutions \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) and \(A_\bullet(\mathcal{U}')\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.3.6 (Standard resolution)

a) Let \(C\) be a small category. For any integer \(n > 0\), we note \(\operatorname{Fl}^n(C)\) the set of sequences of morphisms of \(C\): \((u_1, \dots, u_n)\) such that for all \(i\), \(0 < i < n\), the goal of \(u_i\) is equal to the source of \(u_{i+1}\) so that the morphisms \(u_i\) and \(u_{i+1}\) are composable. Define a semi-simplicial set \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{ES}(C) = \operatorname{ob} C & \operatorname{Fl}^1(C) \ar@<0.5ex>[l] \ar@<-0.5ex>[l] & \operatorname{Fl}^2(C) \ar@<1ex>[l] \ar[l] \ar@<-1ex>[l] & \operatorname{Fl}^3(C) \ar@<1.5ex>[l] \ar@<0.5ex>[l] \ar@<-0.5ex>[l] \ar@<-1.5ex>[l] & \dots \ar[l] } \] whose face operators \(s_i : \operatorname{Fl}^n(C) \to \operatorname{Fl}^{n-1}(C)\) are as follows: \[ \begin{aligned} s_0(u_1, \dots, u_n) &= (u_2, \dots, u_n) , \\ s_i(u_1, \dots, u_n) &= (u_1, \dots, u_{i+1}u_i, \dots, u_n) , \quad 0 < i < n , \\ s_n(u_1, \dots, u_n) &= (u_1, \dots, u_{n-1}) . \end{aligned} \]

b) Show that when \(C\) has an initial object or a final object, the complex \(\operatorname{ES}(C)\) is homotopically trivial.

c) For any object \(X\) of \(C\), we note \({}_X\backslash C\) the category of source arrows \(X\). Define a semi-simplicial prebundle \(\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)\) whose value in any object \(X\) of \(C\) is \(\operatorname{ES}({}_X\backslash C)\). We note \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)}\) the pre-bundle free abelian semi-simplicial generated by \(\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)\). It is provided with a canonical augmentation \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)} \longrightarrow \underline{\mathbf{Z}}\) in constant pre-beam of value \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}\). Show that, by passing to the associated differential complex, the complex \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)}\) is a resolution of \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}\) and that the components of this complex are projective abelian prebundles.

d) For any abelian presheaf \(M\), we set \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(M) = \operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)}, M)\). Explain the components of \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(M)\). Show that for any integer \(q \geqslant 0\), \(H^q(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(M)) = H^q(\widehat{C}, M)\).

e) Show that for any sheaf \(S\) on \(C\) the functors \(H^q(S, \cdot)\) commute to restrictions of scalars.

f) Note that when \(C\) is a group, \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)}\) is the standard resolution of the trivial module \(\mathbf{Z}\) [3].

g) Show that for any abelian presheaf \(M\) on \(C\), \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)} \otimes M\) is a resolution (left) of \(M\). Set the functor \(\varinjlim_C\) on the prebundles. Show that it is correct on the right. Note \(H_q(C, M)\) its left-derived functors. Show that the components of the complex \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)} \otimes M\) are acyclic for \(\varinjlim_C\). Deduce, by noting \(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(M)\) the complex \(\varinjlim_C(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\underline{\operatorname{ES}}(C)} \otimes M)\), isomorphisms \(H_q(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(M)) \sim H_q(C, M)\).

h) Let \(u : \widehat{C} \to \mathrm{Ens}\) be the unique morphism of the topos \(\widehat{C}\) in the topos punctual. We note \(u_! : \widehat{C}_{\mathbf{Z}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\) the adjoint to the left of the functor \(u^* : \mathrm{Ab} \to \widehat{C}_{\mathbf{Z}}\). Show that for any abelian presheaf \(M\), \(u_!M = \varinjlim_C M\).

i) We now note \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(C, M)\) and \(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(C, M)\) the complexes noted \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(M)\) and \(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(M)\). A pre-bundle \(M\) on \(C\) is said to be locally constant if it transforms all morphisms of \(C\) into isomorphisms. Associate with any pre-beam locally constant \(M\) a locally constant presheaf \(\check{M}\) on the category \(C^\circ\) (category opposite to \(C\)) such that for any morphism \(u\) of \(C\), \(\check{M}(u) = M(u)^{-1}\). Find a canonical isomorphism between the complexes \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(C, M)\) and \(\operatorname{ST}^\bullet(C^\circ, \check{M})\) (resp. \(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(C, M)\) and \(\operatorname{ST}_\bullet(C^\circ, \check{M})\)).

j) Let \(C\) be a small category with an initial object (resp. a small filtering category). Show that for any constant presheaf \(M\), \(H^q(\widehat{C}, M) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) (resp. \(H_q(C, M) = 0\) for \(q > 0\)).
2.4 Case of small sites. Čech cohomology.

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site, \(\widetilde{C}\) the topos of the beams on \(C\), \(\varepsilon : C \to \widetilde{C}\) the canonical functor which associates with an object of \(C\) the sheaf associated with the pre-beam represented by this object. By abuse of notation, for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any bundle of \(A\)-modules \(M\) we will put \(H^q(X, M) = H^q(\varepsilon X, M)\) (see 2.3.1). Remember that when the topology of \(C\) is less fine than the canonical topology, which is always the case in practice, the functor \(\varepsilon\) is fully faithful and makes it possible to identify \(C\) with its image by \(\varepsilon\).

We note \(\mathscr{H}^0 : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_A\) the inclusion functor of the sheaves of \(A\)-modules in the pre-harness category of \(A\)-modules. For any bundle of \(A\)-modules \(M\) we have so by definition:

for any object \(X\) of \(C\). The functor \(\mathscr{H}^0\) is left exact. Its right-derived functors are noted \(\mathscr{H}^q\). As for any \(X\) object of \(C\), the functor "section on \(X\)" is exact in the category of pre-bundles, we have
for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any bundle of \(A\)-modules \(M\), so that the pre-bundle \(\mathscr{H}^q(M)\) is none other than the pre-beam \(X \longmapsto H^q(X, M)\).

We assume that \((C, A)\) is a small ringed site so that \(\widetilde{C}\) is a topos to which we can apply the results of 2.3. Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) and \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be a covering sieve. For any pre-bundle of \(A\)-modules \(G\), the groups \(H^q(R, G)\) (which are therefore calculated in the topos \(\widehat{C}\)) are called the Čech cohomology groups of the prebundle \(G\) relative to the sieve covering \(R\).

When \(R \hookrightarrow X\) is the sieve generated by a covering family \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) of quarable morphisms these groups can be calculated using the complex \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, G)\) (2.3.3) called Čech complex of \(G\) relating to the covering family \(\mathcal{U}\) (or recovery \(\mathcal{U}\)). The groups \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, G) = H^q(R, G)\) (2.3.4) are then called Cech cohomology groups of \(G\) relative to the covering family \(\mathcal{U}\).

Let \(M\) be a bundle of \(A\)-modules on \(C\). The groups \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, \mathscr{H}^0(M))\) are most often noted, improperly, \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, M)\), and called Čech cohomology groups of bundle \(M\) relating to the covering family \(\mathcal{U}\).

We note \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^0 : \widehat{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_A\) the natural extension to the prebundles of \(A\)-modules, of the functor \(L\) described in II. We therefore have, by definition, for a pre-bundle \(G\) and a object \(X\) of \(C\):
(2.4.5.1) \[ \check{\mathscr{H}}^0(G)(X) = \varinjlim_{R \hookrightarrow X} G(R) , \]

the inductive limit being taken according to the screens covering \(X\). It follows from (2.4.5.1) that the functor \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^0\) is left-exact. The functors derived to the right of \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^0\) are denoted \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^q\). As the functors "section on \(X\)" and "filtering inductive limit" are exact, it results from (2.4.5.1) that we have
(2.4.5.2) \[ \check{\mathscr{H}}^q(G)(X) = \varinjlim_{R \hookrightarrow X} H^q(R, G) , \]
the limit being taken according to the screens covering \(X\).
Pre-sheaves \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^q\) are called Cech cohomology pre-sheaves. For any object \(X\) of \(C\), we place
(2.4.5.3) \[ \check{H}^q(X, G) = \check{\mathscr{H}}^q(G)(X) . \]
The groups \(\check{H}^q(X, G)\) are called the Čech cohomology groups. When the topology of \(C\) is defined by a pretopology, which is most often the case in practice, we have, considering 2.3.4,
(2.4.5.4) \[ \check{H}^q(X, G) \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathcal{U}} H^q(\mathcal{U}, G) , \]
the inductive limit being taken according to the quarable covering families preordered by the relation of natural order on the sieves which correspond to them (2.3.5).
Let \(M\) be a bundle of \(A\)-modules. We pose, abusively
(2.4.6.1) \[ \check{H}^q(X, M) = \check{H}^q(X, \mathscr{H}^0(M)) , \qquad \check{\mathscr{H}}^q(M) = \check{\mathscr{H}}^q(\mathscr{H}^0(M)) , \]

and the groups \(\check{H}^q(X, M)\) are called Čech cohomology groups of the sheaf \(M\). Let us point out that if the functors \(\check{H}^q\) are functors derived on the category of presheaves, they do not form, in general, a \(\delta\)-functor on the category of sheaves.
2.5 Change of universe. Čech cohomology in the case of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site and \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\). The site \((C, A)\) is then a \(\mathcal{V}\)-site and we have a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), a \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) and a canonical inclusion functor \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\). The functor \(\varepsilon\) is exact and fully faithful to the categories of Modules and transforms injective Modules into injective Modules (1.9). For any pair of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves of \(A\)-modules we therefore have canonical isomorphisms
(2.5.1.1) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}; M, N) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}; \varepsilon M, \varepsilon N) \qquad q \geqslant 0 . \]
In particular, for any \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaf of sets \(R\) over \(C\), we have canonical isomorphisms (2.1.1)
(2.5.1.2) \[ H^q(R, M) \simeq H^q(\varepsilon R, \varepsilon M) \qquad q \geqslant 0 , \]
and more particularly, for any object \(X\) of \(C\), we have canonical isomorphisms (2.4.1)
(2.5.1.3) \[ H^q(X, M) \simeq H^q(X, \varepsilon M) . \]

In vague terms, we can therefore say that the cohomology of the beams does not depend on the choice of universes and we can always, for the necessity of a demonstration or a construction, increase the universe to calculate the cohomology of a sheaf.

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site and \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\). Let us note \(\widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{U}}\) the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-sheaves, \(\hat{\varepsilon} : \widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{U}} \to \widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}\) the inclusion functor of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-sheaves in the \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves of \(A\)-modules. The functor \(\hat{\varepsilon}\) is exact and therefore the functors derived from the functor \(\hat{\varepsilon}\mathscr{H}^0 : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}\) ( ) are the functors \(\hat{\varepsilon}\mathscr{H}^q\), \(q \geqslant 0\). By abuse of notation, we will again denote \(\mathscr{H}^q : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}\), \(q \geqslant 0\), the functors \(\hat{\varepsilon}\mathscr{H}^q\). This enlargement of the universe presents when \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small the following advantage: The category \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is not in general a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and the \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundles of \(A\)-modules are not necessarily submodules of \(\mathcal{U}\)-injective pre-beams, whereas the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-prefasceaux is a topos (a \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos) and that consequently all \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundle of \(A\)-modules is a subobject of an \(\mathcal{V}\)-injective prebundle (0.1.1). So for any \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundle of sets \(R\) (and in particular when \(R\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-harness) and for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-harness of \(A\)-modules \(M\), groups \(H^p(R, \mathscr{H}^q(M))\) are defined by 2.1.1 and it follows from (2.5.1.2) that these groups do not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{V}\) considered. Likewise, for any pair of integers \(\geqslant 0\) \(p\) and \(q\), the presheaves \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^p(\mathscr{H}^q(M))\) are defined by 2.4.5 and it follows from (2.5.1.2) and (2.4.5.4) that these pre-bundles do not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{V}\) used to define them.

pages.title: 3. The Cartan-Leray spectral sequence relating to an overlap

blocks.html: 3. The Cartan-Leray spectral sequence relating to an overlap

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site, \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\). The functor \(\mathscr{H}^0 : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}\) (2.5.2) transforms the \(A\)-Injective modules in injective pre-beams. For any integer \(q > 0\), and any \(A\)-Module \(M\), the bundle associated with pre-bundle \(\mathscr{H}^q(M)\) is zero.

Let us note \(a_{\mathcal{V}}\) the "associated bundle" functor for the \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves and \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widetilde{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}\) the inclusion functor of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves in the \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles. We have \(a_{\mathcal{V}} \mathscr{H}^0 = \varepsilon\) (II 3.6) and like the functors \(a_{\mathcal{V}}\) and \(\varepsilon\) are exact, we have \(a_{\mathcal{V}} \mathscr{H}^q = 0\) for all integer \(q > 0\). For all \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaf \(M\) and all \(\mathcal{V}\)-presheaf \(N\) we have a functorial isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}}(N, \mathscr{H}^0(M)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}}(a_{\mathcal{V}} N, \varepsilon M)\). When \(M\) is injective, \(\varepsilon M\) is injective (1.9) and since the functor \(a_{\mathcal{V}}\) is exact, the functor \(N \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{C}_{A,\mathcal{V}}}(N, \mathscr{H}^0(M))\) is exact. As a result \(\mathscr{H}^0(M)\) is injective.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site, \(R\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundle of sets on \(C\), \(M\) a bundle of \(A\)-Modules. There exists a spectral, functorial sequence in \(R\) and in \(M\): \[ \text{(3.2.1)} \qquad H^{p+q}(aR, M) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(R, \mathscr{H}^q(M)) . \] (When \(C\) is not \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, the term \(H^p(R, \mathscr{H}^q(M))\) should be interpreted as the cohomology of the prebundle \(\mathscr{H}^q(M)\) in the topos \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) where \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(C\) is \(\mathcal{V}\)-small (2.5.2)).

By definition of the "associated sheaf" functor (cf. II) we have an isomorphism of functor \(H^\bullet(aR, M) \simeq H^0(R, \mathscr{H}^0(M))\). The functor \(\mathscr{H}^0\) transforms injective objects into injective objects. The spectral sequence of compound functors (0.3) is the desired spectral sequence.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) and \(\mathcal{U} = (U_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a covering family such that for all \(i \in I\), the morphism \(U_i \to X\) is quarrable. We then have a sequence spectral (known as Cartan-Leray): \[ \text{(3.3.1)} \qquad H^{p+q}(X, M) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathscr{H}^q(M)) . \]

Let \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be the sieve generated by \(\mathcal{U}\). As this screen is covering, the beam associated with \(R\) is the beam associated with \(X\) (II 5.2). Taking into account 2.4.1, we have \(H^{p+q}(aR, M) = H^{p+q}(X, M)\). The corollary then results from 2.3.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

There exists a functorial spectral sequence in the beam \(M\) and the object \(X\) by \(C\) \[ \text{(3.4.1)} \qquad H^{p+q}(X, M) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(X, \mathscr{H}^q(M)) . \]
This spectral sequence provides, when \(X\) varies in \(C\), the spectral sequence of pre-beams:
(3.4.2) \[ \mathscr{H}^{p+q}(M) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = \check{\mathscr{H}}^p(\mathscr{H}^q(M)) . \]
These spectral sequences provide functorial morphisms (corner morphisms):
(3.4.3) \[ \varphi^q(M) : \check{\mathscr{H}}^q(M) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}^q(M) , \]
(3.4.4) \[ \varphi_X^q(M) : \check{H}^q(X, M) \longrightarrow H^q(X, M) . \]

The morphisms \(\varphi^q\) and \(\varphi_X^q\) are isomorphisms when \(q\) equals 0 or 1. Morphisms \(\varphi^2\) and \(\varphi_X^2\) are monomorphisms. More generally, when the pre-beams \(\mathscr{H}^i(M)\) are null for \(0 < i < n\), the morphisms \(\varphi^q(M)\) and \(\varphi_X^q(M)\) are isomorphisms for \(0 \leqslant q \leqslant n\) and monomorphisms for \(q = n + 1\).

The first spectral sequence is obtained by passing to the inductive limit in the spectral sequence (3.2.1) on the \(R \hookrightarrow X\) screens covering \(X\) (2.4.5.2). The second spectral sequence is immediately deduced (2.4.5.3). The beams associated with the pre-beams \(\mathscr{H}^q(M)\) are zero when \(q > 0\) (3.1). We therefore have \(a \circ \mathscr{H}^q(M) = 0\) for all \(q > 0\) (II 3.4). Therefore \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^0 \mathscr{H}^q(M)\) is a separate pre-beam whose associated beam is zero. We therefore have \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^0 \mathscr{H}^q(M) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) (II 3.2). The assertions on the morphisms \(\varphi^q\) and \(\varphi_X^q\) are immediately deduced from this.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos and \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module (on the left to fix the ideas). Let us note \(\underline{M}\) the abelian group underlying \(M\). The functor \(M \longmapsto \underline{M}\) is exact and consequently, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), the canonical isomorphism \[ H^0(X, M) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^0(X, \underline{M}) \] extends into morphisms \[ H^q(X, M) \longrightarrow H^q(X, \underline{M}) \qquad q \geqslant 0 . \] These morphisms are isomorphisms.

For any object \(Y\) of \(E\), we have (2.4.5.4): \[ \check{H}^p(Y, M) = \varinjlim_{\mathcal{U}} H^p(\mathcal{U}, M) \qquad \check{H}^p(Y, \underline{M}) = \varinjlim_{\mathcal{U}} H^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{M}) \] the inductive limit being taken on the epimorphic families \(\mathcal{U} = (Y_i \to Y)\), \(i \in I\).

Consequently, the canonical homomorphism \(\check{H}^p(Y, M) \to \check{H}^p(Y, \underline{M})\) is an isomorphism (2.3.4). Therefore (2.4.5.3), the canonical homomorphism \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^p(M) \to \check{\mathscr{H}}^p(\underline{M})\) is an isomorphism. If \(M\) is an \(A\)-Injective module, we have \(\check{\mathscr{H}}^p(\underline{M}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\); hence \(\mathscr{H}^1(\underline{M}) = 0\) and by induction on \(p\), \(\mathscr{H}^p(\underline{M}) = 0\), \(p > 0\) (3.4). So \(H^p(X, \underline{M}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\) (2.4.2.2) and (2.5). Consequently, the functor \(M \longmapsto \underline{M}\) transforms injective objects into acyclic objects for the functor \(H^0(X, \cdot)\), hence the isomorphism announced.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 3.6

Let \(G\) be a topological group and \(B_G\) its classifying topos (IV 2.5). Let us note \(E_G\) the object of \(B_G\) constituted by the topological space underlying \(G\) provided with the translation operation to left by the elements of \(G\). The canonical morphism of \(E_G\) into the final object \(e_G\) of \(B_G\) is a epimorphism; hence an overlap \(\mathcal{U} = (E_G \to e_G)\) and, for any abelian sheaf \(F\) of \(B_G\), a spectral sequence \[ \text{(3.6.1)} \qquad E_2^{p,q} = H^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathscr{H}^q(F)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(B_G, F) , \] that we propose to study.

1) Show that for any integer \(n\), the topos \[ (B_G) / (E_G \times E_G \times \dots \times E_G) \qquad (n \text{ facteurs}) \] is canonically equivalent to the topos (IV 2.5) \[ \mathrm{TOP}(G \times G \times G \times \dots \times G) \qquad (n-1 \text{ facteurs}) \] For any integer \(n\), let us denote \(F_n\) the sheaf on the topological space \(G \times \dots \times G\) (\(n\) factors) induced by \(F\).

2) Deduce that the term \(E_2^{p,q}\) of (3.6.1) is canonically isomorphic to the \(p\)-th group of cohomology of a complex of the type \[ H^q(e, F_0) \longrightarrow H^q(G, F_1) \longrightarrow H^q(G \times G, F_2) \longrightarrow \dots , \] which we will explain (\(e\) designates the topological space reduced to a point).

3) Deduce that the cohomology of the topos classifying \(B_G\) has value in the sheaves locally constants is isomorphic to the corresponding singular cohomology of the classifying spaces used in topology when for topological spaces of the type \(G \times G \times \dots \times G\), the cohomology of locally constant sheaves coincide with the corresponding singular cohomology (which is the case when, for example, \(G\) is locally contractible); (For classifying spaces, we can consult [15]).
4. Acyclic beams

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(F\) be a \(A\)-Module, \(S\) be a family topologically generating \(E\). We say that \(F\) is \(S\)-acyclic if for every object \(X\) of \(S\), and all \(q > 0\), we have \(H^q(X, F) = 0\). When \(S\) is equal to \(\operatorname{ob} E\), the beams \(S\)-acyclics are called flabby bundles.

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site, \(F\) be a bundle of \(A\)-modules on \(C\). It is said that \(F\) is \(C\)-acyclic if \(F\) is \(S\)-acyclic where \(S\) is the family of beams associated with the objects by \(C\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.3

Let \((C, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site, \(F\) be a bundle of \(A\)-modules. Let us note \(\mathscr{H}^0 : \widetilde{C}_A \to \widehat{C}_A\) the inclusion functor of \(A\)-Modules in the category of pre-sheaves of \(A\)-modules (these are \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves where \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe such that \(C\) or \(\mathcal{V}\)-small). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. i)

    \(F\) is \(C\)-acyclic.

  2. ii)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any sieve covering \(R \hookrightarrow X\), we have: \[ H^q(R, \mathscr{H}^0(F)) = 0 \quad \text{pour tout } q > 0 . \]

  3. iii)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\), we have: \[ \check{H}^q(X, F) = 0 \quad \text{pour tout } q > 0 ; \]

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii): Since \(F\) is \(C\)-acyclic, pre-sheaves \(\mathscr{H}^q(F)\) are zero for \(q > 0\). The spectral sequence 3.2.1 then provides an isomorphism \(H^q(R, \mathscr{H}^0(F)) \simeq H^q(aR, F)\) for all \(q\). Consequently \(H^q(R, \mathscr{H}^0(F)) = 0\) for \(q > 0\).
ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) iii): clear by passing to the inductive limit.
iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i): we prove by induction on \(q\) that the presheaves \(\mathscr{H}^q(F)\) are zero for \(q > 0\). For this, we use 3.4.2.

It follows from criterion 4.3 ii) and 3.5 that the property of \(S\)-acyclicity only depends on the bundle underlying abelian. In particular, a bundle of \(A\)-modules is flaccid if and only if the bundle underlying abelian is flaccid.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos and \(F\) be a \(A\)-Module. The following properties are equivalents:

  1. i)

    \(F\) is flabby;

  2. ii)

    For any epimorphic family \(\mathcal{U} = (X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\), \[ H^q(\mathcal{U}, F) = 0 \quad \text{pour tout } q > 0 . \]
In 4.2, we take for \(C\) the topos \(E\) itself. The corollary then results from the equivalence i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ii) of 4.2.

The injective bundles are flaccid. Flaccid bundles are \(S\)-acyclic for any family topologically generating \(S\). Flaccid bundles are not necessarily injective (take for topos \(E\) the topos of the sets). \(S\)-acyclic bundles are not necessarily flaccid (exercise 4.1.3).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.7

Let \((E, A)\) be a topos, \(F\) be a \(A\)-Flax module, \(X\) be an object of \(E\). For any subobject \(Y\) of \(X\) the canonical homomorphism \(H^0(X, F) \longrightarrow H^0(Y, F)\) is surjective.

Let \(Y\) be a sub-object of \(X\) such that the morphism \(H^0(X, F) \longrightarrow H^0(Y, F)\) is not surjective. Let \(Z\) be the object obtained by gluing two copies \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) of \(X\) along \(Y\). The object \(Z\) is covered by the two sub-objects \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) we have \(X_1 \times_Z X_2 = Y\). Let us note \(\mathcal{U} = (X_1, X_2)\) the recovery thus obtained. We immediately see that \(H^1(\mathcal{U}, F) \neq 0\). Contradiction.

The property described in 4.7 does not characterize, in the case of general topos, the bundles flanges (exer. 4.15). However, it characterizes it in the case of topos generated by their open and in particularly in the case of topos associated with topological spaces (ex. 4.16). Flask terminology adopted here coincides in the case of topological spaces with the terminology of [7] (exer. 4.16).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.9.

Let \(u : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) be a morphism of ringed topos.

  1. 1)

    The functor \(u_*\) transforms flaccid \(A\)-Modules into \(A'\)-Modules flasks.

  2. 2)

    Let \(S\) and \(S'\) be topologically generating families of \(E\) and \(E'\) respectively such as \(u^*(S') \subset S\). The functor \(u_*\) transforms \(A\)-Modules \(S\)-acyclic into \(A'\)-\(S'\)-acyclic modules.

  3. 3)

    When \(u\) is a flat morphism (1.8) the functor \(u_*\) transforms the \(A\)-Injective modules in \(A'\)-Injective modules.

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E'\), \(\mathcal{U} = (X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) an epimorphic family, \(F\) a \(A\)-Flax module, \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, u_* F)\) the Čech complex of the cover \(\mathcal{U}\). We have a canonical isomorphism \(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, u_* F) \simeq C^\bullet(u^* \mathcal{U}, F)\) using addition of \(u_*\) and of \(u^*\) and the fact that \(u^*\) commutes at the inductive limits and consequently \(u^*(\mathcal{U}) = (u^* X_i \to u^* X)_{i \in I}\) is an epimorphic family. As \(F\) is flaccid, we have \(H^q(u^*(\mathcal{U}), F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) and subsequently \(H^q(\mathcal{U}, u_* F) = H^q(C^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, u_* F)) = H^q(C^\bullet(u^* \mathcal{U}, F)) = H^q(u^*(\mathcal{U}), F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\). So \(u_* F\) is flaccid.

Let's demonstrate 2). When \(S'\) is stable by fiber products a demonstration similar to that which above allows us to demonstrate 2). In the general case, we will use the spectral sequence of the morphism \(u\) (5.3.2). Assertion 2) will not be used before 5.3.2. Let \(F\) be a \(S\)-acyclic beam. The beams \(R^q u_* F\) are the beams associated with pre-beams \(X \longmapsto H^q(u^* X, F)\). As \(S'\) is a topologically generating family and as \(F\) is \(S\)-acyclic, we have \(R^q u_* F = 0\) for all \(q > 0\). The spectral sequence 5.3.2 then provides an isomorphism for any object \(X\) of \(E'\): \(H^q(X, u_* F) \simeq H^q(u^* X, F)\) and consequently, for all \(X\) in \(S'\), \(H^q(X, u_* F) = 0\). Let us demonstrate 3). When the morphism \(u\) is flat, the functor \(u^*\) for Modules is exact (1.8). Consequently the functor \(u_*\) appended to the right of \(u^*\) transforms injective objects into injective objects (0.2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.10

Let \(F\) be a \(A\)-Module of the ringed topos \((E, A)\), \(G\) be a \(A\)-Injective module.

  1. 1)

    The functor \(F \longmapsto \mathscr{H}om_A(F, G)\) is exact.

  2. 2)

    The abelian group \(\mathscr{H}om_A(F, G)\) is flaccid.

Proof: Let's show 1). Either: \[ 0 \longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow F'' \longrightarrow 0 \] an exact sequence. We need to show that the following: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_A(F'', G) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_A(F, G) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_A(F', G) \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact and for this it suffices to show that for any object \(H\) of \(E\), the following: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_E(H, \mathscr{H}om_A(F'', G)) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_E(H, \mathscr{H}om_A(F, G)) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_E(H, \mathscr{H}om_A(F', G)) \longrightarrow 0 \] is correct. Now (IV 6.12) this last sequence is isomorphic to the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_A(A_H \otimes_A F'', G) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_A(A_H \otimes_A F, G) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_A(A_H \otimes_A F', G) \longrightarrow 0 \] and the \(A\)-Module \(A_H\) is flat (1.3.1).
2) Let's show that \(\mathscr{H}om_A(F, G)\) is flaccid. Either: \[ \mathcal{U} = (X_i \longrightarrow X) , \quad i \in I , \] an epimorphic family of \(E\), and

\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ C_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) : \dots \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & \coprod_{i,j} \mathbf{Z}_{X_i \times_X X_j} \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \coprod_i \mathbf{Z}_{X_i} . } \] the complex defined in 1.5. This complex is a flat resolution of the object \(\mathbf{Z}_X\) which is itself flat (1.4 and 1.3). Let's then calculate the groups:

\[ H^q(\mathcal{U}, \mathscr{H}om_A(F, G)) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet_{\mathbf{Z}}(C_\bullet(\mathcal{U}), \mathscr{H}om_A(F, G))) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\operatorname{Hom}_A(C_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} F, G)) \]

The complex \[ C_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} F \] is acyclic in degree \(\neq 0\), because \(C_\bullet(\mathcal{U})\) is a flat resolution of a flat module. By continuation \(\operatorname{Hom}(C_\bullet(\mathcal{U}) \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} F, G)\) is acyclic in degree \(\neq 0\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.11

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(F\) be a flaccid (resp. injective) bundle of \(A\)-modules.

  1. 1)

    For any object \(X\) of \(E\) the \(A|X\)-Module \(j_X^* F\) is flaccid (resp. injective).

  2. 2)

    For all closed \(Z\) from \(E\), the beam of sections from \(F\) to supports in \(Z\) (IV 14) is flaccid (resp. injective).

Assertion 1) results from 4.5 when \(F\) is flaccid. The functor \(j_X^*\) admits a left adjoint exact \(j_{X!}\) (IV 11). Consequently, when \(F\) is injective, \(j_X^* F\) is injective (0.2). Let us demonstrate 2). Let us note \(i : Z \to E\) the inclusion morphism. The beam of sections from \(F\) to support in \(Z\) is then the beam \(i_* i^! F\) (IV 14). The functor \(i_* i^!\) is adjoint on the right to the functor \(i_* i^*\) which is exact (IV 14). Subsequently it transforms injective beam into injective beam. Let \(U\) be open complementary to \(Z\), \(j : U \to E\) the inclusion morphism and \(F\) a flaccid sheaf. We have a sequel exact (IV 14):
(4.11.1) \[ 0 \longrightarrow i_* i^! F \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow j_* j^* F . \]

For any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have \(j_* j^* F(X) = F(X \times U)\) and the morphism \(F(X) \longrightarrow j_* j^* F(X)\) induced by the last arrow of (4.11.1) comes from the canonical injection \(X \times U \hookrightarrow X\). As \(F\) is flaccid, this morphism is surjective (4.7) and consequently, the last arrow of 4.11.1 is an epimorphism of pre-sheaves. For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the exact sequence of cohomology deduced from 4.11.1 provides \(H^q(X, i_* i^! F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) and consequently \(i_* i^! F\) is flask.

The property for a bundle to be flaccid (resp. injective) is located (4.11 1)). But this is not not, in general, a property of local character (Ex. 4.15). However, it is a character property local in the case of topos generated by their open and in particular in the case of topos associated with topological spaces (exercis. 4.16).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.13

Let \(X\) be a locally compact space and \(F\) be a \(c\)-soft sheaf [7]. In using the local character of softness (loc. cit.), show that the restriction of \(F\) to any open \(X\) paracompact is a soft beam. Show that paracompact open spaces form a basis of the topology from \(X\). Deduce that, by noting \(S\) the family of paracompact open sets of \(X\), the beam \(F\) is \(S\)-acyclic. Show that the sheaf of continuous functions on \(X\) is \(S\)-acyclic but is not flaccid when \(X\) is not discrete.

blocks.title: Issue
blocks.html:
Problem 4.14

Study totally acyclic topos, i.e. topos such as \(H^q(X, F) = 0\) for any \(q > 0\), any object \(X\), any abelian beam \(F\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.15

Let \(G\) be a discrete group, \(B_G\) its classifying topos (IV 2.4). Show that for any abelian sheaf \(F\) and any monomorphism \(X \hookrightarrow Y\), the homomorphism \(F(Y) \longrightarrow F(X)\) is surjective. Let \(E(G)\) be the group \(G\) considered as a homogeneous space under himself. It is an object of \(B_G\). The topos \(B_{G/E(G)}\) is equivalent to the punctual topos (IV 8). Morphism \(E(G) \longrightarrow e\) (\(e\) final object of \(B_G\)) is an epimorphism. For any abelian beam \(F\) of \(B_G\), beam \(F|E(G)\) is flaccid. Deduce that the property of being flaccid or injective is not local character.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.16

We say that a topos \(E\) is generated by its open objects if the open objects of \(E\) (i.e. the sub-objects of the final object \(e\) of \(E\)) form a generating family (I 7). Such a topos has the property next:

(P) Any epimorphic family \(X_i \longrightarrow X\), \(i \in I\), is increased by a family epimorphic \(U_j \longrightarrow X\), \(j \in J\), where the \(U_j \longrightarrow X\) are monomorphisms.

a) Are there topos which have the property (P) and which are not generated by their open ones? The topos associated with topological spaces are generated by their open spaces. If \(E\) is generated by its open (resp. enjoys (P)), for any object \(X\) of \(E\), \(E_{/X}\) is generated by its open (resp. enjoys of (P)). Any sub-topos of a topos generated by its open is generated by its open. The property (P) is not a local character property.

b) Let \(E\) be a topos, \(\operatorname{Ouv}(E)\) the category of open objects of \(E\) provided with the topology induced. The inclusion functor \(\operatorname{Ouv}(E) \longrightarrow E\) is a morphism of sites, hence a topos morphism \(\Pi : E \longrightarrow \widetilde{\operatorname{Ouv}(E)}\). The functor \(\Pi^*\) is fully faithful. The morphism \(\Pi\) has with respect to the 2-category of topos generated by their open a universal property that the reader will explain.

c) Let \(E\) be a topos generated by its open points and \(\operatorname{Point}(E)\) be the set of points to be isomorphisms near \(E\). Show that \(\operatorname{Point}(E)\) is small. Put a topology on \(\operatorname{Point}(E)\) and define a morphism \(\operatorname{Top}(\operatorname{Point}(E)) \longrightarrow \widetilde{\operatorname{Ouv}(E)}\) making \(\operatorname{Top}(\operatorname{Point}(E))\) a subtopos of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Ouv}(E)}\). For any topological space \(X\), show that any morphism of \(\operatorname{Top}(X)\) in \(E\) provides a morphism of \(\operatorname{Top}(X)\) in \(\operatorname{Top}(\operatorname{Point}(E))\). Show that a topos generated by its open is equivalent to a topos \(\operatorname{Top}(X)\) (\(X\) topological space) if and only if it has enough points. Show that there exist topos generated by their open which are not equivalent to topos \(\operatorname{Top}(X)\) where \(X\) is a topological space.

d) Let \(E\) be a topos having the property (P). For an abelian beam \(F\) on \(E\) to be flaccid, it is necessary and sufficient that for any monomorphism \(X \hookrightarrow Y\), the homomorphism \(F(Y) \longrightarrow F(X)\) is surjective.

e) Let \(E\) be a topos having the property (P). Show that the property for a sheaf \(F\) of being flaccid is a property of local nature.

f) Let \(E\) be a topos generated by its open objects, \(e\) the final object of \(E\). So that an abelian beam \(F\) is flaccid it is necessary and sufficient that for all open \(U\) of \(e\), the canonical morphism \(F(e) \longrightarrow F(U)\) is surjective.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.17 (Flaggy beams and universe change)
