Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (for example a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos) and \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\). Let us note \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \longrightarrow \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the corresponding beam categories and the canonical injection. Let \(F\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-abelian beam flange on \(C\). We propose to show that \(\varepsilon F\) is flaccid. We first notice that for any object \(X\) of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) we have \(H^q(\varepsilon X, \varepsilon F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\). Any object \(Y\) of \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) admits an epimorphic family \(\varepsilon X_i \longrightarrow Y\), \(i \in I\) where the \(\varepsilon X_i \longrightarrow Y\) are monomorphisms. We conclude that \(H^q(Y, \varepsilon F) = \varinjlim{}^q_{\varepsilon X \to Y} F(X)\). For show that these \(\varinjlim^q\) are zero for \(q \neq 0\), we can return to the topos \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Ouv}(C_{\mathcal{V}/Y})}\) and use the fact that for this topos, a beam is flaccid if it is locally.

pages.title: 5. The \(R^q u_*\) and the Cartan-Leray spectral sequence relating to a topos morphism

blocks.html: 5. The \(R^q u_*\) and the Cartan-Leray spectral sequence relating to a topos morphism

Let \(u : (E, A) \to (E', A')\) be a morphism of ringed topos, \(u_* : E \to E'\) the image functor direct. The notation \(u_*\) will still designate the extension to Modules of the direct image functor. The functor \(u_*\) for Modules (on the left to fix the ideas) is exact on the left. Its right derivative functors are noted \(R^q u_*\), \(q \geq 0\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.1
  1. 1)

    For any \(A\)-Module \(M\), the beam \(R^q u_* M\) is the beam associated with pre-beam \(X' \longmapsto H^q(u^* X', M)\) (\(X' \in \operatorname{ob} E'\)).

  2. 2)

    The formation of functors \(R^q u_*\) commutes to the restrictions of scalars.

  3. 3)

    The formation of \(R^q u_*\) switches to locations. In a way specifies, for any object \(X'\) of \(E'\), if we designate by \(u_{/X'} : E_{/u^* X'} \longrightarrow E'_{/X'}\) the morphism deduced from \(u\) by localization (IV 8), we have, for all \(A\)-Module \(M\), a canonical isomorphism

    (5.1.1) \[ R^q(u_{/X'})_* (M | u^* X') \simeq R^q u_*(M) | X' \qquad q \geq 0 . \]

Let us denote by \(\hat{u}_* : \widehat{E} \longrightarrow \widehat{E'}\) the direct image functor for the \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundles (\(\hat{u}_* M = M \circ u^*\)). As \(u^*\) and \(u_*\) are adjoint, we have a isomorphism \[ u_* \simeq a \hat{u}_* \varepsilon , \] where \(a\) is the associated beam functor for \(E'\). As the functors \(a\) and \(\hat{u}_*\) are exact, we have \[ R^q u_* \simeq a \hat{u}_* \mathscr{H}^q , \] which is another way of stating 1). Assertion 2) then results from 1) and 3.5. By definition of morphism \(u_{/X'}\), we have a canonical isomorphism 5.1.1 for \(q = 0\). The general case is deduced by noticing that the location functors (IV 8) are exact and transform injective objects into objects injectives (4.11).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.2

Let \(u : E \longrightarrow E'\) be a topos morphism and \(S'\) be a family generator of \(E'\). The acyclic sheaves \(M\) for the functors \(H^0(u^* X', \cdot)\), \(X' \in S'\), are acyclics for the functor \(u_*\). In particular, flaccid sheaves are acyclic for \(u_*\).
Result of 5.1, 1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.3

Let \(u : E \longrightarrow E'\) be a topos morphism and \(M\) be an abelian group of \(E\). We have a sequel spectral:

(5.3.1) \[ E_2^{p,q} = H^p(E', R^q u_* M) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(E, M) . \]

More generally, for any object \(X'\) of \(E'\), we have a spectral sequence:

(5.3.2) \[ E_2^{p,q} = H^p(X', R^q u_* M) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(u^* X', M) . \]

By definition of the direct and reciprocal image functors, we have an isomorphism \(H^0(X', u_* M) \simeq H^0(u^* X', M)\). The functor \(u_*\) transforms injective objects into flaccid sheaves (4.6 and 4.9). The proposed spectral sequences are therefore spectral sequences of compound functors (0.3).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.4

Let \(u : E \longrightarrow E'\) and \(v : E' \longrightarrow E''\) be two topos morphisms. We have a sequel spectral

\[ R^p v_* R^q u_* \Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(v \circ u)_* . \]

We have \(v_* u_* \simeq (vu)_*\) and the functor \(u_*\) transforms the injective objects into flaccid sheaves (4.9) therefore acyclic for \(v_*\) (5.2). We therefore have a spectral sequence of compound functors (0.3).

pages.title: 6. Ext premises and cohomology with supports

blocks.html: 6. Ext premises and cohomology with supports

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module, on the left to fix the ideas. The functor \(N \longmapsto \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)\), on the category of \(A\)-Modules with left and values in the category of Abelian Groups is exact on the left (IV 12). Its right derivative functors are denoted:
In particular, we have
(6.0.2) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N) = \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N) . \]
By definition, we have canonical isomorphisms (2.1 and IV 12).
(6.0.3) \[ H^0(E, \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N)) = \operatorname{Ext}^0_A(E; M, N) = \operatorname{Hom}_A(M, N) . \]
More generally, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have (2.2)
(6.0.4) \[ H^0(X, \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N)) = \operatorname{Ext}^0_A(X; M, N) = \operatorname{Hom}_{A|X}(M|X, N|X) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.1
  1. 1)

    The formation of functors \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A\) commutes to locations. Precisely, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have functorial isomorphisms:

    (6.1.1) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)|X \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A|X}(M|X, N|X) . \]

  2. 2)

    The sheaf \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)\) is isomorphic to the sheaf associated with pre-beam \(X \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M, N)\).

  3. 3)

    There exists a spectral sequence

    (6.1.2) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_A(E; M, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(E, \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) . \]
More generally, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have a functorial spectral sequence in \(X\) and in them arguments \(M\) and \(N\)
(6.1.3) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_A(X; M, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(X, \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) . \]

By definition, we have an isomorphism (6.1.1) when \(q = 0\) (IV 12). The general case is deduced taking into account due to the fact that the location functors are exact and transform the injective modules into injective modules (2.2). The functor \(N \longmapsto \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N) = \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N)\) transforms the injective modules in flaccid bundles therefore in acyclic bundles for \(H^0(X, \cdot)\) (4.10). The spectral sequences (6.1.2) and (6.1.3) are spectral sequences of compound functors taking into account (6.0.3) and (6.0.4). When \(X\) varies in \(E\), the spectral sequence (6.1.3) provides a spectral sequence of pre-beams, hence passing to the beams associated, a spectral sequence of beams. Like the beams associated with the pre-beams \(X \longmapsto H^p(X, \cdot)\) are zero when \(p \neq 0\) (3.1), this spectral sequence degenerates and provides the isomorphism announced in 2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2

The functors \((M, N) \longmapsto \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)\), \(q \geq 0\), form a \(\delta\)-functor into variable \(M\) and variable \(N\). Explicitly, for any exact sequence \(0 \longrightarrow N' \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow N'' \longrightarrow 0\) (resp. \(0 \longrightarrow M' \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow M'' \longrightarrow 0\)) we have long sequences exact:

(6.2.1) \[ \dots \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N') \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N'') \overset{\delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{E}xt^{q+1}_A(M, N') \longrightarrow \dots \]
(resp.

(6.2.2) \[ \dots \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M'', N) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M', N) \overset{\delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{E}xt^{q+1}_A(M'', N) \longrightarrow \dots ) . \]

It follows from the general properties of the functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^q\) that for any object \(X\) of \(E\), the functors \((M, N) \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M, N)\), \(q \geq 0\), form a \(\delta\)-functor in each of the arguments, hence the assertion, by varying \(X\) and taking the associated sheaf (6.1).

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module, \(Z\) be a closed one of \(E\) (IV 9), \(U\) be the open one complementary. We note \(H^0_Z(E, M)\) the group of sections of \(M\) whose support is contained in \(Z\) (IV 14) and \(\underline{H}^0_Z(M)\) the sub-beam of \(M\) defined by the sections of \(M\) "to supports in \(Z\)" (IV 14). The functors \(H^0_Z(E, \cdot)\) and \(\underline{H}^0_Z(\cdot)\) are left-exact (IV 14). The derived functors are denoted \(H^q_Z(E, \cdot)\) and \(\underline{H}^q_Z(\cdot)\) respectively and called the groups (resp. bundles) of cohomology of \(M\) with support in \(Z\)(∗).
We have canonical isomorphisms (IV 14)
(6.3.1) \[ H^0_Z(E, M) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_A(A_Z, M) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^0_A(E; A_Z, M) , \]
(6.3.2) \[ \underline{H}^0_Z(M) \simeq \mathscr{H}om_A(A_Z, M) \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(A_Z, M) , \]
hence isomorphisms for all \(q \geq 0\)
(6.3.3) \[ H^q_Z(E, M) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(E; A_Z, M) , \]
(6.3.4) \[ \underline{H}^q_Z(M) \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(A_Z, M) . \]
We will notice that \(A_Z\) being a biModule, the beams \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(A_Z, M) \simeq \underline{H}^q_Z(M)\) are canonically equipped with \(A\)-Module structures.

For any object \(X\) of \(E\), let us note \(Z_{/X}\) the closed subtopos of \(E_{/X}\) deduced from \(Z\) by location (it is the complement of \(U \times X\)). By definition, we have canonical isomorphisms
We pose
Given (6.3.2), we have canonical isomorphisms
(6.3.7) \[ H^q_Z(X, M) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; A_Z, M) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.4
  1. 1)

    The formation of functors \(\underline{H}^q_Z\) commutes at the location. Precisely, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), and any \(A\)-Module \(M\), we have isomorphisms canonicals

    (6.4.1) \[ \underline{H}^q_Z(M)|X \simeq \underline{H}^q_{Z_{/X}}(M|X) . \]

  2. 2)

    The beam \(\underline{H}^q_Z(M)\) is the beam associated with the pre-beam \(X \longmapsto H^q_Z(X, M)\).

  3. 3)

    There is a spectral sequence:

    (6.4.2) \[ H^{p+q}_Z(E, M) \Longleftarrow H^p(E, \underline{H}^q_Z(M)) . \]
More generally, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), there exists a spectral sequence
(6.4.3) \[ H^{p+q}_Z(X, M) \Longleftarrow H^p(X, \underline{H}^q_Z(M)) . \]
We are only translating proposition 6.1 using dictionary 6.3.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5

With the notations of 6.3, let us denote \(j : U \to E\) the canonical morphism. For everything \(A\)-Module \(M\), there is an exact sequence of beams:
(6.5.1) \[ 0 \longrightarrow \underline{H}^0_Z(M) \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow j_*(M|U) \longrightarrow \underline{H}^1_Z(M) \longrightarrow 0 , \]
and isomorphisms for \(q \geq 2\):
(6.5.2) \[ \underline{H}^q_Z(M) \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^{q-1}_A(A_U, M) \simeq R^{q-1} j_*(M|U) . \]
We also have a long exact sequence
(6.5.3) \[ \dots \longrightarrow H^q_Z(E, M) \longrightarrow H^q(E, M) \longrightarrow H^q(U, M) \longrightarrow H^{q+1}_Z(E, M) \longrightarrow \dots \](∗),
and more generally, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have a long exact sequence
(6.5.4) \[ \dots \longrightarrow H^q_Z(X, M) \longrightarrow H^q(X, M) \longrightarrow H^q(X \times U, M) \longrightarrow H^{q+1}_Z(X, M) \longrightarrow \dots \]
By definition of \(A_Z\), we have an exact sequence (IV 14)
(6.5.5) \[ 0 \longrightarrow A_U \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow A_Z \longrightarrow 0 . \]

The functors \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(A, \cdot)\) are zero for \(q > 0\). We have \(\mathscr{H}om_A(A_U, M) \simeq j_*(M|U)\) (IV 14) and subsequently (2.2) \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(A_U, M) \simeq R^q j_*(M|U)\). Finally \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(A_Z, M) \simeq \underline{H}^q_Z(M)\) (6.3.4). The long exact sequence (6.2.2) provides in this case (6.5.1) and (6.5.2). The exact sequences (6.5.3) and (6.5.4) result from dictionary 6.3 and the long exact sequence of the \(\delta\)-functor \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; \cdot, M)\), \(q \geq 0\), associated with (6.5.5).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.6
  1. 1)

    Flaccid sheaves are acyclic for functors \(\underline{H}^0_Z\) and \(H^0_Z(X, \cdot)\).

  2. 2)

    The functors \(\underline{H}^q_Z\) and \(H^q_Z(X, \cdot)\) commute to the restrictions of scalars.

Let \(M\) be a flaccid beam. The exact sequence (6.5.4) provides equalities \(H^q_Z(X, M) = 0\) for all \(X\) and all \(q \geq 2\) and an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow H^0_Z(X, M) \longrightarrow H^0(X, M) \longrightarrow H^0(X \times U, M) \longrightarrow H^1_Z(X, M) \longrightarrow 0 . \] But the beam \(M\) being flaccid, the morphism \(H^0(X, M) \longrightarrow H^0(X \times U, M)\) is surjective (4.). Consequently \(H^1_Z(X, M) = 0\) and \(M\) is acyclic for \(H^0_Z(X; M)\). Moving on to the associated beams, we deduce that \(M\) is acyclic for the functor \(\underline{H}^0_Z\) (6.4). It is clear that the functors \(\underline{H}^0_Z\) and \(H^0_Z(X, \cdot)\) commute to restrictions of scalars and like sheaves flaccid are acyclic for these two functors, property 2) results.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.7

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(Z\) a closed one of \(E\), \(U\) the open one complementary, \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-Modules. There are functorial isomorphisms in \(M\) and \(N\) compatible with closed changes:
\[ \underline{H}^0_Z(\mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{H}om_A(M, \underline{H}^0_Z(N)) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{H}om_A(M \otimes_A A_Z, N) . \]
Result of (IV 14) taking into account (6.3.2).
We pose
(6.8.1) \[ \mathscr{H}om_{A, Z}(M, N) = \underline{H}^0_Z(\mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) . \]

The functor \(N \longmapsto \mathscr{H}om_{A, Z}(M, N)\) is left exact. Its derived functors are denoted \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N)\): These are the bundles \(\mathscr{E}xt\) with supports in \(Z\). We have therefore
(6.8.2) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, Z}(M, N) = \mathscr{H}om_{A, Z}(M, N) . \]
Let's set \(M_Z = M \otimes_A A_Z\). It follows from IV 14 that \(M_Z\) is the direct image on \(E\) of the image reciprocal on \(Z\) of \(M\). We therefore have, taking into account 6.7 isomorphisms
(6.8.3) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N) \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M_Z, N) . \]
Now let's move on to global invariants. We pose
(6.8.4) \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{A, Z}(M, N) = \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(E; M, N) = H^0_Z(E, \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) . \]

The group \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A, Z}(M, N)\) is the subgroup of the group of morphisms of \(M\) in \(N\) of which the support is in \(Z\) (IV 14) i.e. which are zero on \(U\). More generally, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), we pose
(6.8.5) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(X; M, N) = H^0_Z(X, \mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)) . \]

Functors \(N \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(X; M, N)\) are left-exact. Derived functors are noted \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_{A, Z}(X; M, N)\). These are the groups \(\operatorname{Ext}\) with supports in \(Z\). Definitions 6.3.6, 6.8.1 and 6.8.5 and isomorphisms 6.7 provide isomorphisms

(6.8.6) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(X; M, N) \simeq \begin{cases} H^0(X, \mathscr{H}om_{A, Z}(M, N)) , \\ \operatorname{Ext}^0_A(X; M, \underline{H}^0_Z(N)) , \\ \operatorname{Ext}^0_A(X; M_Z, N) . \end{cases} \]
The last isomorphism of (6.8.6) provides isomorphisms
(6.8.7) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A, Z}(X; M, N) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; M_Z, N) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.9
  1. 1)

    There are two functorial spectral sequences in \(M\) and \(N\) compatible with closed changes

    (6.9.1) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^{p+q}_{A, Z}(M, N) \Longleftarrow \begin{cases} E_2^{p,q} = \underline{H}^p_Z(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) , \\ {}'E_2^{p,q} = \mathscr{E}xt^p_A(M, \underline{H}^q_Z(N)) . \end{cases} \]

  2. 2)

    There are three functorial spectral sequences in \(X\), \(M\) and \(N\) compatible with closed changes

    (6.9.2) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_{A, Z}(X; M, N) \Longleftarrow \begin{cases} E_2^{p,q} = H^p_Z(X, \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) , \\ {}'E_2^{p,q} = H^p(X, \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N)) , \\ {}''E_2^{p,q} = \operatorname{Ext}^p_A(X; M, \underline{H}^q_Z(N)) . \end{cases} \]

  3. 3)

    The beams \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N)\) are canonically isomorphic to the bundles associated with the pre-sheaves \(X \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A, Z}(X; M, N)\).

When \(N\) is injective, the beam \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M, N)\) is flaccid 4.10 therefore acyclic for \(\underline{H}^0_Z\) (6.6). The first spectral sequence of 6.9.1 is a spectral sequence of compound functors (6.8.1). Likewise, when \(N\) is injective, \(\underline{H}^0_Z(N)\) is injective (4.11) and the second sequence spectral of 6.9.1 is a spectral sequence of compound functors deduced from 6.7. The spectral sequences of 6.9.2 are spectral sequences of compound functors deduced from definition 6.8.5 and the first two isomorphisms of 6.8.6. Finally, by varying \(X\) in the second spectral sequence of 6.9.2 and taking the associated beams, we obtain a spectral sequence of beams which degenerates thanks to 3.1 and which provides the isomorphisms of 3).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.10

The functors \((M, N) \longmapsto \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N)\), \(q \geq 0\), and \((M, N) \longmapsto \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A, Z}(X; M, N)\), \(q \geq 0\), are \(\delta\)-functors in each of the variables \(M\) and \(N\). By noting \(M_U\) the beam \(M \otimes_A A_U\) (cf. IV 11), we have a long sequence exact

(6.10.1) \[ \dots \overset{\delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, Z}(M, N) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M_U, N) \overset{\delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{E}xt^{q+1}_{A, Z}(M, N) \longrightarrow \dots \]
and an analogous exact sequence by the groups \(\operatorname{Ext}\).

The functors \(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(\cdot, \cdot)\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; \cdot, \cdot)\) form \(\delta\)-functors with respect to each of the variables (6.2) and the functor \(M \longmapsto M_Z\) is exact because \(A_Z\) is flat (1.3.3). The first assertion therefore results from isomorphisms 6.8.3 and 6.8.7. The exact sequel 6.10.1 and the analogous exact sequence for the groups \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is the long exact sequence of \(\delta\)-functor \[ \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(\cdot, N) , \quad q \geq 0 \quad (\text{resp. } \operatorname{Ext}^q_A(X; \cdot, N) , \quad q \geq 0) \] relating to the exact sequence \(0 \longrightarrow M_U \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow M_Z \longrightarrow 0\), taking into account from 6.8.3 and 6.8.7.

Let us briefly indicate how we can extend these results to the case of support families. Let \(E\) be a topos. For any object \(X\) of \(E\) let us designate by \(\operatorname{Fer}(X)\) all of the closed objects of the topos \(E_{/X}\). This set is in one-to-one correspondence, by passing to the complementary, with all of the open from the topos \(E_{/X}\), i.e. with all the sub-objects of \(X\) (IV 8). The pre-bundle \(\operatorname{Fer} : X \longmapsto \operatorname{Fer}(X)\) is actually a \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam as it is checks immediately, so it is representable. In other words, there exists an object of \(E\) denoted again \(\operatorname{Fer}\) and a closed \(Z_{\operatorname{Fer}}\) of \(E_{/\operatorname{Fer}}\) such that for all \(X\), any element of \(\operatorname{Fer}(X)\) is deduced from \(Z_{\operatorname{Fer}}\) by a change of base by a morphism \(u_Z : X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Fer}\) only determined by \(Z\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.12

We call support family of \(E\) a subset \(\Phi\) of the set of closed ones of \(E\) which has the following properties:

(S1) The union of a finite family of elements of \(\Phi\) belongs to \(\Phi\).

(S2) Closed everything of \(E\) contained in an element of \(\Phi\) is an element of \(\Phi\).

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(\Phi\) be a support family of \(E\), \(X\) be an object of \(E\). We designate by \(\Phi(X)\) the smallest family of supports of \(E_{/X}\) which contains the closed ones of \(E_{/X}\) deduced from closed from \(\Phi\) by the base change \(X \longrightarrow e\) (\(e\) final object of \(E\)). The functor \(X \longmapsto \Phi(X)\) is a sub-pre-beam of the beam \(\operatorname{Fer}\). It is therefore separated.
We say that a family \(\Phi\) of supports of \(E\) is of local character if it has the property next:

(CL) For any epimorphic family \((X_i \longrightarrow e)\), \(i \in I\) (where \(e\) is the final object of \(E\)) the sequence of sets \[ \Phi \longrightarrow \prod_i \Phi(X_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \Phi(X_i \times X_j) \] is correct.

Let \(a\Phi\) be the beam associated with the pre-beam \(X \longmapsto \Phi(X)\) (6.12.1). The condition (CL) is equivalent to the condition that the canonical morphism \(\Phi \longrightarrow a\Phi(e)\) is a bijection. (cf. the construction of the associated beam in II in the case of a separate pre-beam). To verify (CL) we can therefore limit ourselves to a final family of epimorphic families \((X_i \longrightarrow e)\), \(i \in I\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 6.12.3

1) Let \(Z\) be a closed one of \(E\). The set of closed ones of \(E\) contained in \(Z\) is a family of supports of \(E\). It is local in character.

2) Let \(T\) be a topological space and \(\Phi\) be a family of paracompactifying supports of \(T\) [7]. The family \(\Phi\) is not, in general, local in character.

3) Let \(T\) be a topological space and \(p\) be an integer. The \(\Phi_p\) family of \(T\) closed Krull codimension \(\geq p\), is a family of supports of \(T\). It is local in character.

4) Let \(E\) be a topos having the following property: any epimorphic family \((X_i \longrightarrow e)\), \(i \in I\), is augmented by a finite epimorphic family. So every family of supports of \(E\) is of local character. Indeed, using example 1), it suffices to show that a limit inductive filtering \(\Phi_\lambda\) of families of local character is a family of local character, which results immediately from the passage to the inductive limit on the sequence of sets \[ \Phi_\lambda \longrightarrow \prod_i \Phi_\lambda(X_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \Phi_\lambda(X_i \times X_j) , \] where \((X_i \longrightarrow e)\), \(i \in I\), is a finite epimorphic family. Indeed according to 6.12.2 and the hypothesis on \(E\), we can limit ourselves to finite epimorphic families to verify the conditions (CL) and as the filtering inductive limits commute to finite projective limits (I 2), the exactness of these sequences of sets is preserved by passing to the inductive limit.

5) Let \(E\) be a coherent topos (VI 2.3). Then according to the above any support family of \(E\) is of local character.
Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\Phi\) be a support family of \(E\), \(N\) be a \(A\)-Module (left to fix the ideas), \(X\) an object of \(E\). We pose
(6.13.1) \[ \underline{H}^0_\Phi(N) = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} \underline{H}^0_Z(N) , \]
(6.13.2) \[ H^0_\Phi(E, N) = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} H^0_Z(E, N) . \]
Let \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module on the left, we set:
(6.13.3) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, \Phi}(M, N) = \mathscr{H}om_{A, \Phi}(M, N) = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, Z}(M, N) , \]
(6.13.4) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, \Phi}(X; M, N) = \operatorname{Hom}_{A|X, \Phi(X)}(M|X, N|X) = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(X; M, N) . \]
We have canonical isomorphisms:
(6.13.5) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, \Phi}(M, N) \simeq \underline{H}^0_\Phi(\mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N)) , \]
(6.13.6) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, \Phi}(X; M, N) \simeq H^0_\Phi(X, \mathscr{E}xt^0_A(M, N)) . \]
When \(\Phi\) is the family of closed objects contained in a closed \(Z\), we have isomorphisms:

(6.13.7) \[ \begin{cases} \underline{H}^0_\Phi \simeq \underline{H}^0_Z , \\ H^0_\Phi \simeq H^0_Z , \\ \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, \Phi}(\cdot, \cdot) \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, Z}(\cdot, \cdot) , \\ \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, \Phi}(X; \cdot, \cdot) \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, Z}(X; \cdot, \cdot) . \end{cases} \]
The inductive limits which define the preceding functors are filtering. Consequently these functors are exact on the left. Their right derivative functors are denoted:
(6.13.8) \[ \underline{H}^q_\Phi , \quad H^q_\Phi , \quad \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, \Phi}(\cdot, \cdot) , \quad \operatorname{Ext}^q_{A, \Phi}(X; \cdot, \cdot) . \]
They are also calculated by inductive limits of the functors \(\underline{H}^q_Z\), \(H^q_Z\), etc., for \(Z\) browsing \(\Phi\).
From isomorphisms 6.13.5 and 6.13.6, we draw two spectral sequences by passing to the inductive limit on the first spectral sequence of 6.9.1 and the first spectral sequence of 6.9.2:
(6.13.9) \[ \mathscr{E}xt^{p+q}_{A, \Phi}(M, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = \underline{H}^p_\Phi(\mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) , \]
(6.13.10) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_{A, \Phi}(X; M, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p_\Phi(X, \mathscr{E}xt^q_A(M, N)) . \]
With the notations of 6.13, we have, without hypothesis on \(\Phi\), a functorial morphism
(6.14.1) \[ \theta : H^0_\Phi(N) \longrightarrow H^0(E, \underline{H}^0_\Phi(N)) . \]

This morphism is always injective but is not in general an isomorphism. However, if \(\Phi\) is local character, the morphism \(\theta\) is an isomorphism as is immediately verified. Likewise if \(\Phi\) is local, we have an isomorphism
(6.14.2) \[ \theta' : \operatorname{Ext}^0_{A, \Phi}(E; M, N) \simeq H^0(E, \mathscr{E}xt^0_{A, \Phi}(M, N)) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.15

Let \((E, A)\) be a ringed topos, such that \(E\) is coherent (VI 2.3), \(\Phi\) a support family of \(E\), \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-Modules. There are two spectral sequences:
(6.15.1) \[ H^{p+q}_\Phi(E, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p_\Phi(E, \underline{H}^q_\Phi(N)) , \]
(6.15.2) \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_{A, \Phi}(E; M, N) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(E, \mathscr{E}xt^q_{A, \Phi}(M, N)) . \]

These spectral sequences are deduced from the spectral sequence 6.4.2 and from the second spectral sequence 6.9.2 by passage to the inductive limit on the closed \(Z\) of \(\Phi\), taking into account that the cohomology of a topos coherent commutes at inductive beam limits (IV 5).

Let us point out, without demonstration, that we can extend the second spectral sequence to the case of support families of 6.9.1 and the third spectral sequence of 6.9.2 (with \(X\) = final object of \(E\)) assuming that the topos \(E\) is consistent and the Module \(M\) is perfect [13].
Finally we can also generalize the notion of families of supports by introducing the pre-bundles of support families, sheaves of support families and cohomology groups and sheaves correspondents(∗).

footnotes.html: (∗) Compare with SGA 2 I for the case of ordinary topological spaces, as well as the presentation by HARTSHORNE cited p.80 below.
(∗) this exact sequence specifies the role of global cohomological invariants \(H^q_Z(E, N)\) as "cohomology groups of \(E\) modulo the open \(U\), with coefficient in \(M\)".
(∗) cf. [12] ch. IV, § 1 (Lecture Notes 20, Springer) for the development of these notions in the form of a fugue with variations.

blocks.html:
7. Appendix: Čech

cohomology

By developing an idea due to P. Cartier, we show in this paragraph how we can in a topos arbitrary, calculate the cohomology of a sheaf using overlaps. For the classical theory of paracompact spaces, we refer to [7]; for another method which applies to certain topos, and in particular to the flat topos, see [14].
7.1 Skeleton and coskeleton

Let \(\Delta\) be the category of typical simplexes (the objects of \(\Delta\) are the ordered sets \(\Delta_n = [0, \dots, n]\), the morphisms are the increasing applications). Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and \(\Delta E\) the category of pre-bundles on \(\Delta\) with value in \(E\), in other words the category of semi-simplicial objects of \(E\). Let us denote by \(\Delta[n]\) the subcategory full of \(\Delta\) defined by the objects \(\Delta_p\), \(p \leq n\), by \(i_n : \Delta[n] \to \Delta\) the functor of inclusion and by \(\Delta E[n]\) the category of pre-bundles on \(\Delta[n]\) to value in \(E\), otherwise said, the category of semi-simplicial objects truncated to the order \(n\) of \(E\). According to I 5.1, we have a sequence of three adjoint functors (I 5.3)
\[ \xymatrix@C=4em{ \Delta E[n] \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{i_{n!}} \ar@<-1ex>[r]_-{i_{n*}} & \Delta E \ar[l]|-{i_n^*} } \]

where the functor \(i_n^*\) is the functor restriction to the category \(\Delta[n]\), and where the functors \(i_{n*}\) and \(i_{n!}\) are his deputies on the right and left respectively. Note that \(\Delta E\) and \(\Delta E[n]\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (IV 1.2) and \((i_n^*, i_{n*})\) is a topos morphism (IV 3.1) which is an embedding of \(\Delta E[n]\) in \(\Delta E\) (I 5.6 and IV 9.1.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1.1

We note \(\operatorname{sk}_n\) (resp. \(\operatorname{cosk}_n\)) and we call skeleton functor of order n (resp. coskeleton functor of order n) the functor \(i_{n!} i_n^*\) (resp. \(i_{n*} i_n^*\)) from \(\Delta E\) in \(\Delta E\).
The skeleton and coskeleton functors are in constant use in semi-simplicial set theory [3].

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.1.2
  1. 1)

    The addition morphism \(\operatorname{sk}_n \longrightarrow \operatorname{id}\) is a monomorphism. Canonical morphisms \(\operatorname{sk}_n \operatorname{sk}_m \longrightarrow \operatorname{sk}_n\) (resp. \(\operatorname{cosk}_n \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_n \operatorname{cosk}_m\)) are isomorphisms when \(n \leq m\) (resp. \(n \geq m\)).

  2. 2)

    Let \(u : E \longrightarrow E'\) be a topos morphism. The functor \(u^*\), extended to semi-simplicial and truncated semi-simplicial objects, commutes to functors \(i_{n!}\), \(i_n^*\), \(\operatorname{sk}_n\), \(\operatorname{cosk}_n\).

The first assertion follows immediately from the definitions. To demonstrate 2), it suffices to note that, according to I 5.1, the functors \(i_{n!} \dots\), \(\operatorname{cosk}_n\) are calculated using limits inductive and finite projective limits.
7.2 An acyclicity lemma

Let \(M\) be an abelian group of \(E\). The homology of a semi-simplicial object \(K_\bullet\) of \(E\), to coefficients in \(M\), is defined in the usual way: We first consider \(A(K)\), the group free abelian semi-simplicial generated by \(K\), then we form the tensor product \(M \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} A(K)\); we thus obtain an abelian semi-simplicial group of \(E\). We then consider the abelian group complex associated (by forming the alternating sum of the edge operators) and we take the homology. Homology objects are noted \(H_i(K, M)\). These are abelian groups of \(E\). When \(M\) is the group \(\mathbf{Z}_E\) (free abelian group generated by the final object of \(E\)), we note more simply \(H_i(K)\). Groups \(H_i(K)\) are called the homology groups of \(K\). We say that a semi-simplicial object \(K\) is acyclic if for any abelian group \(M\) of \(E\), the groups \(H_i(K, M)\) are zero (\(i > 0\)) or, this which is equivalent, if the groups \(H_i(K)\) are null (\(i > 0\)).
The formation of homology commutes to reciprocal images by topos morphisms.
The goal of this number is to prove the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.2.1

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(K_\bullet\) and \(L_\bullet\) two semi-simplicial objects of \(E\), \(v_\bullet : K_\bullet \longrightarrow L_\bullet\) a morphism of semi-simplicial objects, \(n\) an integer \(\geq 0\). We assume that the morphism \(v_\bullet\) has the properties:

  1. 1)

    For any integer \(p < n\) (\(p \geq 0\)) the morphism \(v_p : K_p \longrightarrow L_p\) is an isomorphism.

  2. 2)

    The morphism \(K_n \overset{v_n}{\longrightarrow} L_n\) is a epimorphism.

  3. 3)

    The canonical morphisms \(K_\bullet \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_n(K_\bullet)\), \(L_\bullet \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_n(L_\bullet)\) are isomorphisms.

Then, for any integer \(p\), the morphism \(v_p\) is an epimorphism and the morphism \(v_\bullet\) induces an isomorphism on homology objects.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

The demonstration shows in fact that by adopting a suitable definition of homotopy in topos, the morphism \(v_\bullet\) is a homotopy equivalence.

Proof: We can assume that \(E\) is the beam topos on a small site \(C\) (IV 1) and that consequently \(E\) is a sub-topos of a topos \(E'\) having sufficient fiber functors (by example the topos \(\widehat{C}\)). Let us denote \(a^* : E' \longrightarrow E\) the inverse image functor by the morphism embedding \(E \hookrightarrow E'\). Either \[ v_\bullet[n] : K_\bullet[n] \longrightarrow L_\bullet[n] \] the morphism of truncated semi-simplicial objects obtained by truncating \(v_\bullet\) to the order \(n\). Let us note \(L_\bullet[n]'\) the image (in \(E'\)) of \(K_\bullet[n]\) by \(v_\bullet[n]\), \(v_\bullet[n]' : K_\bullet[n] \longrightarrow L_\bullet[n]'\) the morphism induced by \(v_\bullet[n]\). Let's set \(L! = i_{n*} L_\bullet[n]'\), \(K! = i_{n*} K_\bullet[n]\), \(v! = i_{n*} v_\bullet[n]'\) (the functor \(i_{n*}\) is here relative to \(E'\)). According to 1), the morphism \(v! : K! \longrightarrow L!\) has the properties 1), 2), 3) of the lemma. Moreover, according to 2), 3) and 7.1.2, the morphism \(a^* v!\) is none other than \(v_\bullet\). It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate the lemma for \(v!\) and consequently we can assume that \(E\) has sufficient fiber functors; therefore, in using these fiber functors, we can reduce ourselves to the case where \(E\) is the category of sets.

We first notice that the hypotheses of the lemma on the morphism \(v_\bullet\) are stable for all change of bases \(M_\bullet \longrightarrow L_\bullet\) where \(M_\bullet\) is a coskeleton of order \(n\). By following the fiber \(P_\bullet\) of \(v_\bullet\) at any base point of \(L_\bullet\) is of the type \(i_{n*} P_\bullet[n]\) where \(P_\bullet[n]\) is a non-empty complex truncated to the order \(n\) of the form
(7.2.2) \[ P_n \longrightarrow e \longrightarrow e \longrightarrow e \longrightarrow \dots \longrightarrow e . \]

Let us note, for any integer \(i\), \(\delta \Delta_i\) the semi-simplicial set edge of the simplex \(\Delta_i\). Any morphism of \(\delta \Delta_i\) in \(P_\bullet\) extends into a morphism of \(\Delta_i\) in \(P_\bullet\). Indeed, the property is obvious if \(i \geq n\) because \(P_\bullet\) is a coskeleton of order \(n\) and it is clear for \(i < n\) according to description 7.2.2. As \(P_\bullet\) is a Kan complex, \(P_\bullet\) is homotopically trivial [6]. Note that the morphism \(v_\bullet\) is a fibration in the sense of Kan [6]. As the fibers of this fibration are homotopically trivial, \(v_\bullet\) induces an isomorphism on the homotopy groups of \(K_\bullet\) and \(L_\bullet\) at all the base points of \(K_\bullet\). According to the Hurewitz's theorem, this implies that \(v_\bullet\) induces an isomorphism on the homology [6]. This demonstrates the second assertion of the lemma. To demonstrate the first assertion, we notice that any morphism of a complex truncated at the order \(n\) in \(L_\bullet[n]\) rises to a morphism in \(K_\bullet[n]\). Subsequently, according to 3), any morphism of a complex in \(L_\bullet\) is raised to a morphism in \(L_\bullet\). So \(v_\bullet\) is surjective.
7.3. Hyper-overlaps

Let \(C\) be a site, belonging to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\), where fiber products and products of two objects are representable. Let us denote by \(\widehat{C}\) the topos of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-beams on \(C\) and by \(\widetilde{C}\) the topos of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(C\). An object \(K_\bullet\) from \(\widehat{C}\) is said to be semi-representable if it is isomorphic to a direct sum of representable presheaves.

Let \(\operatorname{SR}(C)\) be the full subcategory of \(\widehat{C}\) defined by the objects semi-representable. In category \(\operatorname{SR}(C)\) projective limits for categories of finite non-empty indices are representable, and the inclusion functor \(\operatorname{SR}(C) \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}\) commutes to these finite projective limits. From a remark already made, we deduce that if \(K_\bullet\) is a semi-simplicial object truncated to the order \(n\) of \(\widehat{C}\) of which all objects are semi-representable, the extension \(i_n^*(K_\bullet)\) has the same property. So if \(K_\bullet\) is a semi-simplicial object of \(\widehat{C}\) of which all objects are semi-representable, then for all \(n\), the object \(\operatorname{cosk}_n(K_\bullet)\) has the same property.
7.3.1 Definitions and notation

Let \(p > 0\) be an integer. A semi-simplicial object \(K_\bullet\) of \(\widehat{C}\) is called a hyper-overlapping of type \(p\) of \(C\), or when no confusion results, a hyper-overlap of type \(p\), if it has the following properties:
HR1) For any integer \(n \geq 0\), \(K_n\) is semi-representable.
HR2) The canonical morphism \(K_\bullet \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_p(K_\bullet)\) is a isomorphism.

HR3) For any integer \(n \geq 0\) the canonical morphism of presheaves: \(K_{n+1} \longrightarrow (\operatorname{cosk}_n(K_\bullet))_{n+1}\) is a covering morphism of presheaves (II 6.2). Morphism canonical prebundles \(K_o \longrightarrow e\), where \(e\) is the final object of \(\widehat{C}\), is a covering morphism of presheaves.

A hyper-overlap of type \(p\) is a hyper-overlap of type \(q\) for all \(q \geq p\). An object semi-simplicial \(K_\bullet\) will be called a hyper-covering (of \(C\)) if it has the properties HR 1) and HR3).
We will designate by \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. \(\operatorname{HR}\)) and we will call category of hyper-overlaps of type \(p\) (resp. category of hyper-overlaps) the following category:
a) The objects of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. \(\operatorname{HR}\)) are hyperoverlaps of type \(p\) (resp. hyper-overlaps).

b) Let \(K_\bullet\) and \(L_\bullet\) be two objects of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. \(\operatorname{HR}\)). A morphism of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. \(\operatorname{HR}\)) of source \(K_\bullet\) and goal \(L_\bullet\) is a morphism \(v_\bullet : K_\bullet \longrightarrow L_\bullet\) of semi-simplicial objects of \(\widehat{C}\).

Let \(C\) be a site where fiber products can be represented and \(X\) be an object of \(C\). A hyper-overlap of \(X\) (resp. a hyper-overlap of type \(p\) of \(X\)) will be a hyper-overlap of the site \(C/X\) (resp. a hyper-overlap of type \(p\) of \(C/X\)). We similarly define the category of hyper-overlaps of \(X\) (resp. hyper-overlap of type \(p\) of \(X\)). We have a sequence of functors include:
\[ \dots \hookrightarrow \operatorname{HR}_p \hookrightarrow \operatorname{HR}_{p+1} \hookrightarrow \dots \hookrightarrow \operatorname{HR} \]
These functors are fully faithful. We will note \(\operatorname{HR}_\infty\) the inductive limit of categories \(\operatorname{HR}_p\).

Let us designate, for any integer \(n \geq 0\), by \(\underline{\Delta}_n^e\) the semi-simplicial object type of dimension \(n\) with value in the category of sets, i.e. the functor on \(\Delta\) represented by the ordered set \([o, n]\). We denote by \(\underline{\Delta}_n^c\) the pre-beam on \(C\) (at value in the category of semi-simplicial sets) constant of value \(\underline{\Delta}_n\). The pre-bundle \(\underline{\Delta}_n^c\) is therefore a semi-simplicial assembly prebundle. The two canonical injections of \(\Delta_o\) in \(\Delta_1\) define two semi-simplicial presheaf morphisms:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \underline{\Delta}_0^c \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{e_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{e_1} & \underline{\Delta}_1^c } \]
and define, consequently, for any semi-simplicial prebundle \(K_\bullet\), two injections canonicals:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ K_\bullet \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{e_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{e_1} & K_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c } \]

Two morphisms \(\xymatrix@C=2em{K_\bullet \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{u_1} & L_\bullet}\) of Semi-simplicial presheaves are called homotopic morphisms if there exists a morphism \[ v : K_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \longrightarrow L_\bullet \] such as diagrams:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ K_\bullet \ar[r]^-{e_i} \ar[dr]_-{u_i} & K_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \ar[d]^-{v} \\ & L_\bullet } \qquad i = 0, 1 \]

be commutative. The morphism \(v\) is called a homotopy connecting \(u_0\) to \(u_1\). The relation: \(u_0\) and \(u_1\) are two homotopic morphisms of \(K_\bullet\) in \(L_\bullet\), is not, in general, an equivalence relation. However, the equivalence relation generated by this relation is compatible with the composition of morphisms. This therefore allows us to define the category of pre-bundles semi-simplicials up to homotopy, passing to the quotient by the equivalence relation generated by the relation homotopy.
We thus define the categories \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}_p\), \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}_\infty\), \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}\), categories of hyper-overlaps up to homotopy.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.3.2

Let \(C\) be a site satisfying the 7.3.0 conditions.

  1. 1)

    The category \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}_p^\circ\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}^\circ\)) is filtering (I 2.7).

  2. 2)

    Let \(K_\bullet\) be an object of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)), \(n\) an integer such as \(0 \leq n \leq p\) (resp. \(0 \leq n\)), and \[ u : X \longrightarrow K_n \] a covering morphism of presheaves. There is an object \(L_\bullet\) of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)) and a morphism \(f : L_\bullet \longrightarrow K_\bullet\), such that the morphism \[ f_n : L_n \longrightarrow K_n \] factors by \(u\).

  3. 3)

    The semi-simplicial bundles associated (II 3.5) with hyper-overlaps are acyclic (7.2.0) in strictly positive degrees. The \(0\)-th homology bundle is isomorphic to the sheaf associated with the constant sheaf \(\mathbf{Z}\).

Proof: Let's prove 3). Let \(\widetilde{K}_\bullet\) be the associated semi-simplicial bundle to \(K_\bullet\). The "associated bundle" functor commutes to the functor \(\operatorname{cosk}_n\) (7.1.2. 2)). By following the semi-simplicial bundle \(\widetilde{K}_\bullet\) has the following properties:
a) The canonical morphism \(\widetilde{K}_0 \longrightarrow e\) is an epimorphism of sheaves.
b) For any integer \(n \geq 0\), the canonical morphism \(\widetilde{K}_{n+1} \longrightarrow (\operatorname{cosk}_n(\widetilde{K}_\bullet))_{n+1}\) is an epimorphism of sheaves.
Let us then put, for \(n \geq 0\), \(L_{\bullet n} = \operatorname{cosk}_n \widetilde{K}_\bullet\). We then have, for all \(n\), a sequence of semi-simplicial sheaf morphisms:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \widetilde{K}_\bullet \ar[r]^-{u_n} & L_{\bullet n} \ar[r]^-{v_n} & L_{\bullet\, n-1} \ar[r] & \dots \ar[r]^-{v_0} & e . } \]

and for all \(j\), \(0 \leq j \leq n\), \(v_j\) has the properties of 7.2.1. Additionally, the morphism \(u_n\) induces an isomorphism on the components of degree \(\leq n\). We then deduce from 2.1 by induction on \(n\), that \(\widetilde{K}_\bullet\) is acyclic.
To demonstrate 1) and 2) we will introduce terminology.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.3.3

Let \(f : X_\bullet \longrightarrow Y_\bullet\) be a morphism of semi-simplicial presheaves. It is said that \(f\) is special of type \(p\) (resp. special) if:

  1. 1)

    Objects \(X_\bullet\) and \(Y_\bullet\) are canonically isomorphic to their coskeletons of order \(p\) and \(\operatorname{cosk}_p(f) = f\) (resp. no conditions on \(f\), \(X^\bullet\) and \(Y^\bullet\)).

  2. 2)

    For any integer \(n\) such that \(0 \leq n \leq p\) (resp. \(0 \leq n\)), the morphism \(\phi_{n+1}\) appearing in the diagram below is covering:

    \[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X_{n+1} \ar[rr]^-{f_{n+1}} \ar[dr]|-{\phi_{n+1}} \ar[dd] & & Y_{n+1} \ar[dd] \\ & P_{n+1} \ar[ur]|-{p_{n+1}} \ar[dl] & \\ (\operatorname{cosk}_n X_\bullet)_{n+1} \ar[rr]_-{(\operatorname{cosk}_n f)_{n+1}} & & (\operatorname{cosk}_n Y_\bullet)_{n+1} . } \]

    (The vertical arrows are defined by the canonical morphisms \(X_\bullet \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_n X_\bullet\) and \(Y_\bullet \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_n Y_\bullet\). The object \(P_{n+1}\) is the fiber product and \(\phi_{n+1}\) is the single arrow making the diagram commutative).

  3. 3)

    The morphism \(f_0 : X_0 \longrightarrow Y_0\) is covering.

A semi-simplicial pre-bundle \(K_\bullet\) is said to be special of type \(p\) (resp. special) if the canonical morphism: \[ K_\bullet \longrightarrow e \] (\(e\) is the final semi-simplicial object) is special of type \(p\) (resp. special), i.e. if \(K_\bullet\) meets conditions HR 2) and HR 3) (resp. HR 3)) of 7.3.1.1.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3.4
  1. 1)

    The compound of two special morphisms (resp. special types \(p\)) is a special morphism (resp. special of type \(p\)).

  2. 2)

    Let \(K_\bullet\) be a special semi-simplicial prebundle (resp. special of type \(p\)), \(X_\bullet \overset{f}{\longrightarrow} Y_\bullet\) a special morphism (resp. special of type \(p\)) and \(u : K_\bullet \longrightarrow Y_\bullet\) a morphism of complexes. So the fiber product \(P_\bullet = K_\bullet \times_{Y_\bullet} X_\bullet\) is special (resp. special of type \(p\)).
The proof of this lemma is left to the reader.

Proof of the assertion 2) of 7.3.2.: We can first assume that \(X\) is semi-representable. For any semi-simplicial object \(L_\bullet\), let us designate by \(\operatorname{Hom}_{(X)}(L_\bullet, K_\bullet)\) the set of morphisms of semi-simplicial objects provided with a factorization \(L_n \longrightarrow X \overset{u}{\longrightarrow} K_n\). The functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{(X)}(\cdot, K_\bullet)\) is representable. In indeed this functor transforms inductive limits into projective limits, and the category \(\Delta(\widehat{C})\) is a topos. We will designate by \(P\) an object which represents this functor. For an object \(Z\) of \(\widehat{C}\), let us similarly designate by \(j_n^*(Z)\) the object which represents the functor \(L_\bullet \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom}(L_n, Z)\) on \(\Delta \widehat{C}\), whose construction by \(\varprojlim\) is explained in III 1.1. We see here that this construction only involves finite \(\varprojlim\), from which we conclude as soon as \(j_n^*\) transforms semi-representable presheaves into semi-simplicial presheaves with components semi-representable, and covering morphisms \(Z' \longrightarrow Z\) in special morphisms (7.3.3).
By definition of \(P_\bullet\), we have a Cartesian square:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ P_\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & j_n^*(X) \ar[d]^-{j_n^*(u)} \\ K_\bullet \ar[r] & j_n^*(K_n) } \]

According to what we have just pointed out, the semi-simplicial objects \(j_n^*(X)\) and \(j_n^*(K_n)\) are semi-representable and the morphism \(j_n^*(u)\) is a special morphism of type \(n\). It is then sufficient to conclude to apply 3.4. and sorite 7.3.0.

Let \(M_\bullet\) and \(N_\bullet\) be two semi-simplicial pre-sheaves. The functor: \[ L_\bullet \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom}(L_\bullet \times M_\bullet, N_\bullet) \] is representable. The semi-simplicial prebundle which represents it will be noted \[ \mathscr{H}om_\bullet(M_\bullet, N_\bullet) . \] The component prebeam of degree \(n\) of this object will be denoted \(\mathscr{H}om_n(M_\bullet, N_\bullet)\). We have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathscr{H}om_n(M_\bullet, N_\bullet) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{H}om_0(M_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_n^c, N_\bullet) . \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3.6

Either

\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ M_\bullet \ar[r] & N_\bullet \\ \operatorname{sk}_n \underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[u] & \underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \ar[u] } \qquad (u \text{ l'injection canonique}) \]

a co-Cartesian diagram of semi-simplicial pre-sheaves. For any hyper-overlap \(L_\bullet\), the morphism: \[ \mathscr{H}om_0(N_\bullet, L_\bullet) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_0(M_\bullet, L_\bullet) \] is covering.
Proof. We have a Cartesian diagram:

\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ \mathscr{H}om_0(N_\bullet, L_\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{H}om_0(M_\bullet, L_\bullet) \ar[d] \\ \mathscr{H}om_0(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c, L_\bullet) \ar[r]^-{(*)} & \mathscr{H}om_0(\operatorname{sk}_n \underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c, L_\bullet) } \]

It is therefore sufficient to show that the morphism \((*)\) is covering. But the morphism \((*)\) is isomorphic to morphism: \[ L_{n+1} \longrightarrow (\operatorname{cosk}_n L_\bullet)_{n+1} \] which is covering by hypothesis, c.q.f.d.

Proof of the assertion 1) of 7.3.2. : The product of two objects \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)) is still an object of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)) (7.3.4). To demonstrate that the category \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}_p^\circ\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}^\circ\)) is filtering, it suffices to show that given two morphisms of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. \(\operatorname{HR}\))
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{K_\bullet \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{u_1} & L_\bullet} \]
there exists a morphism \(v : M_\bullet \longrightarrow K_\bullet\) of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)) such as morphisms:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{M_\bullet \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u_0 v} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{u_1 v} & L_\bullet} \]
are homotopic, i.e. such that it exists \(w : M_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \longrightarrow L_\bullet\) making diagrams commutative
\[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ M_\bullet \ar[r]^-{e_i} \ar[d]_-{v} & M_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \ar[d]^-{w} \\ K_\bullet \ar[r]_-{u_i} & L_\bullet } \qquad i = 0, 1 . \]

Let then, for any semi-simplicial object \(M_\bullet\) of \(\widehat{C}\) (not necessarily a hyper-overlap), \(F(M_\bullet)\) the set of couples \((v, w) : v : M_\bullet \longrightarrow K_\bullet\), \(w : M_\bullet \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \longrightarrow L_\bullet\) such that the diagrams \((*)\) are commutative. The functor \(M_\bullet \longmapsto F(M_\bullet)\) is a contravariant functor in \(M_\bullet\) which transforms inductive limits into projective limits, and which is therefore representable by an object semi-simplicial \(F_\bullet\). The object \(F_\bullet\) is obviously the vertex of a Cartesian diagram:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ F_\bullet \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \ar[d]^-{\pi} \\ K_\bullet \ar[r]_-{u_0, u_1} & L_\bullet \times L_\bullet } \]

where \(\pi\) is defined by the two inclusions of \(\underline{\Delta}_0^c\) in \(\underline{\Delta}_1^c\). He It is therefore sufficient, to demonstrate the assertion, to show that \(F_\bullet\) is an object of \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) (resp. of \(\operatorname{HR}\)) and for this, according to 7.3.4.2) and sorite 7.3.0, it suffices to show that
a) \(\mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet)\) is semi-representable,
b) the morphism \(\pi\) is special of type \(p\) (resp. special).

We first immediately check that \(\mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet)\) is a semi-simple, semi-representable object. In fact the components of this object are calculated by limits finite projectives from \(L_n\), and consequently are semi-representable. Now let's check b). Everything first we show that, when \(L_\bullet\) is special of type \(p\), the morphism \[ \mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \longrightarrow \operatorname{cosk}_p \mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \] is an isomorphism; which makes it possible to verify property 1) of 7.3.3. Then, the morphism: \[ \pi_0 : \mathscr{H}om_0(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \longrightarrow L_0 \times L_0 \] is isomorphic to the canonical morphism \[ L_1 \overset{(d_0, d_1)}{\longrightarrow} L_0 \times L_0 \] which is covering by hypothesis (\(L_\bullet\) is hyper-covering). It therefore remains to verify property 2) of 7.3.3, i.e. to verify that \(\forall n\), in the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=2em@R=2.5em{ \mathscr{H}om_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \ar[rr]^-{\pi_{n+1}} \ar[dr]|-{\phi_{n+1}} \ar[dd] & & L_{n+1} \times L_{n+1} \ar[dd] \\ & P_{n+1} \ar[ur] \ar[dl] & \\ (\operatorname{cosk}_n \mathscr{H}om_\bullet(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet))_{n+1} \ar[rr]_-{(\operatorname{cosk}_n \pi)_{n+1}} & & (\operatorname{cosk}_n L_\bullet)_{n+1} \times (\operatorname{cosk}_n L_\bullet)_{n+1} } \]

(\(P_{n+1}\) is the fiber product), the morphism \(\phi_{n+1}\) is covering. Or a “deputy functors chasing” simple shows that: \[ P_{n+1} \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{H}om_0(\operatorname{sk}_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c), L_\bullet) , \] \[ \mathscr{H}om_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{H}om_0(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) , \] and that the morphism \(\phi_{n+1}\) is isomorphic to the morphism \[ \mathscr{H}om_0(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c, L_\bullet) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}om_0(\operatorname{sk}_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c), L_\bullet) \] from the injection \[ \operatorname{sk}_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c) \hookrightarrow \underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c \] Now there exists a sequence of sub-objects of \(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c\): \[ \operatorname{sk}_{n+1}(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c) = M_0 \hookrightarrow M_1 \hookrightarrow \dots \hookrightarrow M_k = \underline{\Delta}_{n+1}^c \times \underline{\Delta}_1^c , \] such that for all \(o \leq i \leq k\), the morphism \[ M_i \hookrightarrow M_{i+1} \] fits into a co-Cartesian diagram:
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ M_i \ar[r] & M_{i+1} \\ \operatorname{sk}_{n+1} \underline{\Delta}_{n+2}^c \ar[r] \ar[u] & \underline{\Delta}_{n+2}^c \ar[u] } \]

(We add one after the other the non-degenerate simplices of dimension \(n+2\) of \(\underline{\Delta}_{n+1} \times \underline{\Delta}_1\)). According to 7.3.6 the morphism \(\phi_{n+1}\) is a composite of covering morphisms and hence is itself covering, which completes the proof of 7.3.2.
7.4. The isomorphism theorem

Let \(F\) be a pre-bundle in abelian groups on a site \(C\) satisfying condition 7.3.0. For everything hyper-covering \(K_\bullet\), we will ask: \[ H^q(K_\bullet, F) = H^q(\operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(K_\bullet, F)) \] The \(H^q(K_\bullet, F)\) form a \(\delta\)-functor on the category of abelian pre-sheaves on \(C\), but they are not, in general, functors derived from the functor \(H^0(K_\bullet, F)\).
Let's then ask:
(7.4.0.1) \[ {}^r\check{H}^q(C, F) = \varinjlim_{\operatorname{HR}_r} H^q(\cdot, F) \qquad (r \text{ peut être infini}) , \]
and
(7.4.0.2) \[ {}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, F) = \varinjlim_{\operatorname{HR}} H^q(\cdot, F) . \]

The \({}^r\check{H}^q(C, F)\) (resp. \({}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, F)\)) still form a \(\delta\)-functor because the category \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}_p^\circ\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{HR}}^\circ\)) is filtering (7.3.2). As \(\operatorname{HR}_p\) categories are not necessarily \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, these \(\delta\)-functors have values in the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-abelian groups, for a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) suitable.

Now suppose that the pre-beam \(F\) is a beam. Like the semi-simplicial bundle associated with a hyper-covering is acyclic (7.3.2), we have a functorial spectral sequence in \(F\) and in \(K_\bullet\):
(7.4.0.3) \[ H^p(K_\bullet, \mathscr{H}^q(F)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\widetilde{C}, F) . \]
Hence, passing to the limit, spectral sequences:
(7.4.0.4) \[ {}^r\check{H}^p(C, \mathscr{H}^q(F)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\widetilde{C}, F) , \] \[ {}^\infty\check{H}^p(C, \mathscr{H}^q(F)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\widetilde{C}, F) . \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.4.1

Let \(C\) be a site satisfying condition 7.3.0, \(F\) an abelian beam on \(C\).

  1. 1)

    Spectral sequences (7.4.0.4) define isomorphisms \[ {}^r\check{H}^q(C, F) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\widetilde{C}, F) , \qquad q \leq r+1 \] and a monomorphism \[ {}^r\check{H}^{r+2}(C, F) \longrightarrow H^{r+2}(\widetilde{C}, F) . \]

  2. 2)

    We have an isomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors: \[ {}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, F) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\widetilde{C}, F) \qquad (\text{pour tout } q) . \]

  3. 3)

    Let \(G\) be a presheaf of abelian groups and \(F\) be the sheaf associated. There are isomorphisms of functors: \[ {}^r\check{H}^q(C, G) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\widetilde{C}, F) , \qquad q \leq r-1 , \] and a monomorphism: \[ {}^r\check{H}^r(C, G) \longrightarrow H^r(\widetilde{C}, F) . \] When \(G\) is a separate presheaf, this last morphism is an isomorphism and there exists a monomorphism: \[ {}^r\check{H}^{r+1}(C, G) \longrightarrow H^{r+1}(\widetilde{C}, F) . \]

  4. 4)

    We have isomorphisms: \[ {}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, G) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} {}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, F) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} H^q(\widetilde{C}, F) \qquad (\text{tout } q) . \]

Proof. : It suffices to show that if \(N\) is an abelian presheaf whose beam associated is zero, we have \({}^r\check{H}^q(C, N) = 0\) for \(q \leq r\) (resp. \({}^\infty\check{H}^q(C, N) = 0\) for all \(q\)); which is done immediately using 3.2.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 7.4.2

1) Note that for sites satisfying the condition of 7.3.0, hyper-overlaps of type \(0\) are the ordinary coverings and the functors \({}^0\check{H}^q\) are none other than the functors \(\check{H}^q\) introduced in (2.4.5.1).

2) Let \(C\) be a site with finite projective limits representable such that for any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any covering family \((X_i \longrightarrow X)\), \(i \in I\), there exists a \(i_0 \in I\) such that \(X_{i_0} \longrightarrow X\) is covering. We can then show that the hyper-overlaps of type \(p\) whose components are representable are cofinal in \(\operatorname{HR}_p\). Likewise, hyper-overlaps \(K_\bullet\) such that there exists an integer \(p\) such that \(K_\bullet\) is of type \(p\) and such that the components of \(K_\bullet\) are representable, are cofinal in \(\operatorname{HR}\). (This can be demonstrated by drawing inspiration from 3.5). These hyper-coverings with representable components are therefore sufficient, in the case envisaged, to calculate the cohomology of the beams associated with the pre-beams.

pages.title: 8. Appendix. Local inductive limits. (by P. Deligne)

blocks.html: 8. Appendix. Local inductive limits. (by P. Deligne)

The editor recommends that the reader avoid, in principle, reading this appendix. He presents a technique which sometimes allows to extend to topos not having enough points assertions that the existence of points makes trivial. This technique makes it possible to obtain a bundletic variant of D. Lazard's theorem affirming that the flat modules on a ring are the inductive limits of free modules of finite type.
8.1. Locally filtering categories
If \(\mathcal{B}\) is a category and if \(p : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) is a category on \(\mathcal{B}\), we will use the following notation
– For \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\), \(\mathcal{A}_U\) is fiber category \(p^{-1}(U)\)

– For \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(\lambda, \mu \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}\) such as \(p(\lambda) = U\) and \(p(\mu) = V\), we set \[ \operatorname{Hom}_f(\lambda, \mu) = p^{-1}(f) , \quad \text{où } p : \operatorname{Hom}(\lambda, \mu) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(U, V) . \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.1

Let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a site. We call locally co-filtering category (or locally filtering left) on \(\mathcal{B}\) a category \(p : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\) such that:

(\(\mathcal{L}\)0) Whatever \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(\mu \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_V\), there exists \(\lambda \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_U\) such that \(\operatorname{Hom}_f(\lambda, \mu) \neq \emptyset\),

(\(\mathcal{L}\)1) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\) and the finite family \((\lambda_i)\) of objects of \(\mathcal{L}_U\), there is an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) and objects \(\mu_j \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_{U_j}\) such that for all \(i\) and \(j\), we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_{f_j}(\mu_j, \lambda_i) \neq \emptyset\),

(\(\mathcal{L}\)2) Whatever \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and the double arrow \((\phi_0, \phi_1) : \lambda \rightrightarrows \mu\) above \(f\), there is an overlap \(f_j : V_j \longrightarrow V\) of \(V\) and goal arrows \(\psi_j\) \(\lambda\) such as \(p(\psi_j) = f_j\) and that \(\phi_0 \psi_j = \phi_1 \psi_j\).
This definition is simplified when \(\mathcal{L}\) is fiberized on \(\mathcal{B}\): the axiom (\(\mathcal{L}\)0) is then satisfied, and (\(\mathcal{L}\)1), (\(\mathcal{L}\)2) can be stated:

(\(\mathcal{L}\)'1) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\) and the finite family \(\lambda_i\) of objects from \(\mathcal{L}_U\), locally on \(U\), there is \(\mu\) sending to all \(\lambda_i\).

(\(\mathcal{L}\)'2) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\), any double arrow \((\phi_0, \phi_1) : \lambda \rightrightarrows \mu\) in \(\mathcal{L}_U\) can, locally on \(U\), be equalized by a goal arrow \(\lambda\).

Let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a site, \(\mathcal{L}\) be a category on \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(\mathcal{C}\) be a field on \(\mathcal{B}\). We denote by \(\Gamma\mathcal{C}\) the category of global sections \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\mathrm{cart}}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C})\) from \(\mathcal{C}\). If \(\mathcal{B}\) has a final object \(S\), \(\Gamma\mathcal{C}\) “is none other” than \(\mathcal{C}_S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.2.1

The category of local projective systems of objects of \(C\), indexed by \(\mathcal{L}\) is the category \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{C})\).

Let us denote by \(c\) (“associated constant local projective system”) the compound functor \[ \Gamma\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Hom}^{\mathrm{cart}}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}) \xrightarrow{u \mapsto u \circ p} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{C}) \] When we need to explain the dependency in \(\mathcal{L}\), we will instead write \(c_{\mathcal{L}}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.3

The local projective limit functor, denoted \(\varprojlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}}\), is the partially defined functor right-adjoint to the functor \(c\).

The functor \[ \varprojlim_{\mathcal{L}} : \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{B}}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{C}) \longrightarrow \Gamma\mathcal{C} \] so check \[ \operatorname{Hom}(X, \varprojlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} X_\lambda) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(cX, (X_\lambda))_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.4

A \(\mathcal{B}\)-functor \(F : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}\) between categories locally cofiltering on \(\mathcal{B}\) is said to be cofinal if it satisfies both conditions following:

(C\(\mathcal{F}\)1) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\) and \(\mu \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{M}_U\), there exists an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) and objects \(\lambda_j \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_{U_j}\) such as \(\operatorname{Hom}_{f_j}(F(\lambda_j), \mu) \neq \emptyset\)

(C\(\mathcal{F}\)2) Whatever \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and the double arrow \((\phi_0, \phi_1) : F(\lambda) \rightrightarrows \mu\) above \(f\), there is an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) and arrows \(\psi_j\) of goal \(\lambda\) in \(\mathcal{L}\) such that \(p(\psi_j) = f_j\) and \(\phi_0 F(\psi_j) = \phi_1 F(\psi_j)\).
The composite of two cofinal functors is a cofinal functor; category equivalences locally filters are cofinal (the proof is left to the reader).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.5

Let \(F : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}\) be a cofinal functor of categories locally cofiltering on \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathcal{C}\) a field on \(\mathcal{B}\) and \((X_\mu)_{\mu \in \mathcal{M}}\) a local projective system indexed by \(\mathcal{M}\). Then, the morphism of functors in \(X\) of \(\Gamma\mathcal{C}\) in (ens): \[ F^* : \operatorname{Hom}(c_{\mathcal{M}}(X), (X_\mu)_{\mu \in \mathcal{M}}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(c_{\mathcal{L}}(X), X_{F(\lambda)})_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} \] is an isomorphism, so that \[ F^* : \varprojlim_{\mathcal{M}} X_\mu \longrightarrow \varprojlim_{\mathcal{L}} X_{F(\lambda)} \] is an isomorphism, both members being simultaneously defined or undefined.
The proof uses the following lemma, left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.1.6

If \(F : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}\) is cofinal, then whatever \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and the arrows above \(f\) \(\phi_i : F(\lambda') \longrightarrow \mu\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) there is an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\), objects \(\lambda'_j \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_{U_j}\) and commutative diagrams

\[ \xymatrix@R=1.5em{ & F(\lambda_1) \ar[dr]^-{\phi_1} & \\ F(\lambda'_j) \ar@{-->}[ur] \ar@{-->}[dr] & & \mu \\ & F(\lambda_2) \ar[ur]_-{\phi_2} & } \]
\[ \xymatrix@C=3em{U_j \ar[r]^-{f_j} & U \ar[r] & V} \]
projection \((f_j, f)\) into \(\mathcal{B}\).

Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\) and \(\mu \in \mathcal{M}_U\), it exists by (C\(\mathcal{F}\)1) an overlay \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) and arrows \(\phi_j : F(\lambda_j) \longrightarrow \mu\) above the \(f_j\). If \[ \psi = (\psi_\lambda) \in \operatorname{Hom}(c_{\mathcal{L}} X, (X_{F(\lambda)})_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}}) : \psi_\lambda : X | p(\lambda) \longrightarrow X_{F(\lambda)} \] is image of \[ \phi = (\phi_\mu) \in \operatorname{Hom}(c_{\mathcal{M}} X, (X_\mu)_{\mu \in \mathcal{M}}) \quad \phi_\mu : X | p(\mu) \longrightarrow X_\mu , \] the diagrams \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=3em{ & X_{F(\lambda_j)} \ar[dr]^-{\phi_j} & \\ X|U_j \ar[ur]^-{\psi_{\lambda_j}} \ar[rr]_-{\phi_\mu|U_j} & & X_\mu|U_j } \] are commutative, so that the \(\phi_\mu\) are determined, locally, by \(\psi\); since \(\mathcal{C}\) is a field, we conclude that \(F^*\) is injective. To prove \(F^*\) surjective, let's start from \(\psi\) and construct \(\phi\) such that \(\psi = F^* \phi\). The previous construction provides the arrows composed: \[ \phi_{\mu, j} : \phi_j \circ \psi_{\lambda_j} : X | U_j \longrightarrow X_\mu | U_j . \] Let's check that these arrows stick together, so that there exists \(\phi_\mu : X | U \longrightarrow X_\mu\) such that \(\phi_\mu | U_j = \phi_{\mu, j}\). Consider a commutative diagram in
\[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=2em{ & U_{ij} \ar[dl]_-{g_i} \ar[dr]^-{g_j} & \\ U_i \ar[dr]_-{f_i} & & U_j \ar[dl]^-{f_j} \\ & U & } \]

To verify that \(\phi_{\mu, j} | U_{ij} = \phi_{\mu, i} | U_{ij}\), simply do it locally on \(U_{ij}\), which allows, according to 8.1.6 and (\(\mathcal{L}\)2), to be limited to the case where there is a diagram commutative
\[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=2em{ & F(\lambda_{ij}) \ar[dl]_-{F(a_{ij})} \ar[dr]^-{F(a_{ji})} & \\ F(\lambda_i) \ar[dr]_-{\phi_i} & & F(\lambda_j) \ar[dl]^-{\phi_j} \\ & \mu & } \]
above the previous one. The diagram
\[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=2.5em{ & X_{F(\lambda_{ij})} \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ X_{F(\lambda_i)} \ar[dr] & X|U_{ij} \ar[u] \ar[l] \ar[r] & X_{F(\lambda_j)} \ar[dl] \\ & X_\mu & } \]
is then commutative, and there exists \((\phi_\mu)\) such that, whatever \(\phi : F(\lambda) \longrightarrow \mu\) above \(f : V \longrightarrow U\), the diagram
\[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=3em{ & X_{F(\lambda)} \ar[dr]^-{\phi} & \\ X|V \ar[ur]^-{\psi_\lambda} \ar[rr]_-{\phi_\mu|V} & & X_\mu|V } \]

be commutative. If \(\sigma : \mu \longrightarrow \mu'\) is an arrow of \(\mathcal{M}\), we will have \((\sigma | V)(\phi_\mu | V) = (\sigma | V) \phi \cdot \psi_\lambda = (\sigma \phi | V) \cdot \psi_\lambda = \phi_{\mu'} | V\), and hence, \(\phi = (\phi_\mu)\) is a functor morphism such that \(\psi = F^*\) as required.

If \(E\) is an ordered set, we will still designate by \(E\) the category, having \(E\) as set of objects, such that \(\operatorname{Hom}(i, j)\) is reduced to \(1\) element or empty depending on whether \(i \leq j\) or not.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.7

Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a locally co-filtering category on a site \(\mathcal{B}\). There is a set ordered \(E\) and a functor \(F\) of \(E\) in \(\mathcal{L}\) such that

(i) \(E\), viewed as category on \(\mathcal{B}\) using \(p^F : E \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), is locally co-filtering.

(ii) The functor \(F\) is cofinal.

Let \(\mathcal{L}'\) be the locally co-filtering product category of \(\mathcal{L}\) and the defined category by the ordered set \(\mathbf{Z}\). The projection of \(\mathcal{L}'\) into \(\mathcal{L}\) is cofinal, so that it is enough to prove (8.1.7) for \(\mathcal{L}'\).

If \(X\) is a finite set of arrows in \(\mathcal{L}'\), we will designate by \(X^\circ\) the set of ends of arrows in \(X\). Let \(E\) be the set of non-empty finite parts of \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{L}')\), such as \(\lambda_X \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}'\) checking
a) no arrow from \(X\), except \(1_{\lambda_X}\), leads to \(\lambda_X\),
b) if \(\mu \in X^\circ\), then \(1_\mu \in X\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(\lambda_X, \mu) \cap X \neq \emptyset\),
c) Any diagram of the type
\[ \xymatrix@R=2em{ \lambda_X \ar[r] \ar[dr] & \mu \ar[d] \\ & \mu' } \]
of arrows of \(X\) is commutative.

We order \(E\) by the relation opposite to the inclusion relation. If \(X \in E\), and if \(\mu \in X^\circ\), it exists one and only one arrow from \(\lambda_X\) in \(\mu\) which is in \(X\). If \(X, Y \in E\) and \(X \supset Y\), that is \(\lambda_{X,Y}\) the single arrow in \(X\) from \(\lambda_X\) to \(\lambda_Y\). The functions \(X \longmapsto \lambda_X\) and \((X \supset Y) \longmapsto \lambda_{X,Y}\) form a functor of \(E\) in \(\mathcal{L}'\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.1.8

The category \(E\) is locally co-filtering on \(\mathcal{B}\) and the functor \(\lambda : E \longrightarrow \mathcal{L}'\) is cofinal.
The axioms must be verified successively:

(i) axiom (\(\mathcal{L}\)0). Let \(f : V \longrightarrow U\) and \(X \in E_U\). There are \(\lambda \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}'_V\) and \(\phi \in \operatorname{Hom}_f(\lambda, \lambda_X)\). In addition, you can choose \(\lambda\) such as \(\lambda \notin X^\circ\) (here serves \(\mathcal{L}' = \mathcal{L} \times \mathbf{Z}\)). Either \(X' = X \cup \{1_\lambda\} \cup Y\) where \(Y\) is the set of composite arrows \[ \lambda \overset{\phi}{\longrightarrow} \lambda_X \overset{\psi}{\longrightarrow} \mu \quad (\psi \in X) . \] So, \(X' \in E\), \(\lambda_{X'} = \lambda\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(X', X) \neq \emptyset\).

(ii) axiom (\(\mathcal{L}\)1). Let \(X_i\) be a finite family of objects of \(E_U\). There is a recovery \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\), objects \(\lambda_j \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}'_{U_j}\) and arrows \(\phi_{ji} \in \operatorname{Hom}_{f_j}(\lambda_j, \lambda_{X_i})\). If \(x \in X_a \cap X_b^\circ\), it exists for each \(j\) an overlap \(g_{jk} : U_{jk} \longrightarrow U_j\) and arrows \(\psi_{jk} : \lambda_{jk} \longrightarrow \lambda_j\) such as \(p(\psi_{jk}) = g_{jk}\) and which equalize the double arrow \(\lambda_j \rightrightarrows x\), where one arrow belongs to \(X_a\), the other to \(X_b\). We can arrange so that the \(\lambda_{jk}\) do not belong to any of the \(X_i^\circ\). Let's replace the \((f_j, \lambda_j, \phi_{ji})\) with the \((f_j g_{jk}, \lambda_{jk}, \phi_{ji} \psi_{jk})\), and repeat this construction for all the \(x\) in a intersection of \(X_i\). We obtain a new system \((f_j, \lambda_j, \phi_{ji})\); let's put \[ X_j = \bigcup_i X_i \cup \{1_{\lambda_j}\} \cup Y_j \] where \(Y_j\) is the set of composite arrows \[ \lambda_j \overset{\phi_{ji}}{\longrightarrow} \lambda_{X_i} \overset{\psi}{\longrightarrow} x \quad (\psi \in X_i) . \] So, \(X_j \in E_{V_j}\), and these \(X_j\) check (\(\mathcal{L}\)1).
(iii) axiom (\(\mathcal{L}\)2). Trivial, for lack of non-trivial double arrows!
(iv) axiom (C\(\mathcal{F}\)1). Trivial, because the functor \(\lambda\) is surjective.

(v) axiom (C\(\mathcal{F}\)2). Let \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) and a double arrow \((\phi_0, \phi_1) : \lambda_X \rightrightarrows \lambda\) above \(f\). There is an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) and arrows \(\psi_j : \lambda_j \longrightarrow \lambda_X\) such that \(p(\psi_j) = f_j\), \(\phi_0 \psi_j = \phi_1 \psi_j\) and \(\lambda_j \notin X^\circ\). Let \(X_j = X \cup \{1_{\lambda_j}\} \cup Y_j\), where \(Y_j\) is the set of composite arrows \[ \lambda_j \overset{\psi_j}{\longrightarrow} \lambda_X \overset{\psi}{\longrightarrow} \mu \quad (\psi \in X) \] Then, \(X_j \in E_{V_j}\), and \(F(\lambda_{X_j, \lambda}) = \psi_j\) equals \((\phi_0, \phi_1)\).

Suppose that \(\mathcal{B}\) is (the site of non-empty open spaces of) the topological space reduced to a point. A locally co-filtering category on \(\mathcal{B}\) is then none other than a left-filtering category (i.e. such that \(\mathcal{B}^\circ\) is filtering in the sense of I 2.7), the cofinal functors corresponding to the cofinal functors of filtering categories on the left (I 8). Proposition 8.1.7 is reformulated

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.9

For any co-filtering category \(\mathcal{L}\) there exists a co-filtering ordered set \(E\) and a cofinal functor of \(E\) in \(\mathcal{L}\).
In this particular case, the proof of 8.1.7 is essentially reduced to the proof of (8.1.9) given in I 8.

Let \(q : \mathcal{C} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a site morphism and \(p : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a locally co-filtering category on \(\mathcal{B}\). Let us designate by \(\mathcal{L}^*\) the category next:

(i) an object of \(\mathcal{L}^*\) is a quadruple \((V, U, \phi, \lambda)\) such that \(V \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{C}\), \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\), \(\phi \in \operatorname{Hom}(V, q^* U)\) and \(\lambda \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{L}_U\)

(ii) an arrow \(f : V, U, \phi, \lambda \longrightarrow V', \phi', U', \lambda'\) is a triple \((f_1, f_2, F_3)\) with \(f_1 \in \operatorname{Hom}(V, V')\), \(f_2 \in \operatorname{Hom}(U, U')\), \(f_3 \in \operatorname{Hom}(\lambda, \lambda')\) and \(q^* f_2 \circ \phi = \phi' \circ f_1\), \(p(f_3) = f_2\).
The functor \(f \longmapsto f_1\) makes \(\mathcal{L}^*\) a category on \(\mathcal{C}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.1.11

The category \(\mathcal{L}^*\) is locally co-filtering on \(\mathcal{C}\).
The axiom (\(\mathcal{L}\)0) is trivial. Let's check (\(\mathcal{L}\)1):

Let us consider a finite family \(L_i = (V, U_i, \phi_i, \lambda_i)\). The \(\phi_i\) define a morphism of \(V\) in the bundle \(q^* a \prod U_i = a \prod q^* U_i\), so that there are commutative diagrams
\[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=3em{ V_j \ar[r]^-{f_j} \ar[d]_-{\psi_j} & V \ar[d]^-{\phi_i} \\ q^* U_j \ar[r]_-{q^*(P_{ij})} & q^* U_i } \]
such that \(f_j\) overlaps \(V\).

Let \(\lambda_{ji}\) verify \(\operatorname{Hom}_{P_{ij}}(\lambda_{ji}, \lambda_i) \neq \emptyset\). According to (\(\mathcal{L}\)1), even if it means refining \(U_j\) (and \(V_j\)), we can take \(\lambda_{ji} = \lambda_j\) independent of \(i\), and the \(L_j = (V_j, U_j, \psi_j, \lambda_j)\) check (\(\mathcal{L}\)1).

Let's check (\(\mathcal{L}\)2). Let \((f_1, f_2) : L^1 \rightrightarrows L^2\), with \(L^i = (V^i, U^i, \phi^i, \lambda^i)\) and \(f_i = (f, g_i, h_i)\). The \(\phi_i\) define a morphism of \(V\) in the bundle \(q^* a \operatorname{Ker}(U^1 \rightrightarrows U^2) = a \operatorname{Ker}(q^* U^1 \rightrightarrows q^* U_2)\), from so that there exist commutative diagrams

\[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=3em{ V_j \ar[r]^-{f_j} \ar[d]_-{\psi_j} & V^1 \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\phi^1} & V^2 \ar[d]^-{\phi^2} \\ q^* U_j \ar[r]_-{p_j} & q^* U^1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & U^2 } \qquad (g_1 p_j = g_2 p_j) , \]

such that \(f_j\) overlaps \(V\). Let \(\chi_j : \lambda_j \longrightarrow \lambda^1\) be such that \(p(\chi_j) = p_j\). According to (\(\mathcal{L}\)2) applied to \((\lambda_1 \chi_j, h_2 \chi_j)\), even if it means refining \(U_j\) (and \(V_j\)), we can manage so that \(h_1 \chi_j = h_2 \chi_j\), and the \(L_j = (V_j, U_j, \psi_j, \lambda_j)\) check (\(\mathcal{L}\)2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.12

Let \(t^* : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a locally co-filtering category on \(\mathcal{B}\), and equip \(\mathcal{A}\) with the topology induced by that of \(\mathcal{B}\) using \(t^*\). Then, \(t^*\) is an equivalence of sites \(t : \mathcal{B} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}^*\).

By construction, \(t_* : \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{F} \circ t^*\) transforms beams to \(\mathcal{B}\) in bundles on \(\mathcal{A}^*\). According to (\(\mathcal{L}\)0), and (\(\mathcal{L}\)1) applied to the empty family, all "fairly small" object of \(\mathcal{B}\) is in the image of \(t^*\) (i.e. the image of \(t^*\) is a sieve covering in \(\mathcal{B}\)), therefore by the "comparison lemma" (III 4) the inclusion in \(\mathcal{B}\) of its full subcategory defined by \(t^*(\operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^*)\) is an equivalence of sites, this which allows us to assume \(t^*\) surjective on objects.
Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a beam on \(\mathcal{A}^*\).

(i) Let \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) be an overlap in \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\lambda^1\) and \(\lambda^2\) in \(\mathcal{A}^*_U\) and \(f_j^i \in \operatorname{Hom}_{f_j}(\mu_j, \lambda^i)\) for \(i = 1, 2\). So, \(\mathcal{F}(\lambda^1)\) and \(\mathcal{F}(\lambda^2)\) have same image in \(\prod_j \mathcal{F}(\mu_j)\).

Let in effect be \(x = (x_j)\) in the image of \(\mathcal{F}(\lambda^1)\). So that \(x\) is in that of \(\mathcal{F}(\lambda^2)\), it is sufficient that for any commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=2em{ \mu \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mu_j \ar[d] \\ \mu_k \ar[r] & \lambda^2 } \] \(x_j\) and \(x_k\) have same image in \(\mathcal{F}(\mu)\). Since \(\mathcal{F}\) is a bundle, we just need to check this after replacing \(\mu\) with the different objects of one of its overlays; according to (\(\mathcal{L}\)2), this allows us to assume diagrams similar to the previous one, with \(\lambda^2\) replaced by \(\lambda^1\), also commutative, in which case the assertion is obvious.

(ii) Let \(f : U \longrightarrow V\) in \(\mathcal{A}^*\), \(\lambda \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^*_U\) and \(\mu \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^*_V\). There then exists an overlap \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\), an object \(\mu' \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^*_U\) and arrows \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=3em{ \lambda_j \ar[r]^-{\phi_j} \ar[d]_-{\psi_j} & \lambda \\ \mu \ar[r]_-{\psi} & \mu' } \] on \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{U_j \ar[r]^-{f_j} & U \ar[r]^-{f} & V} \]
