According to (i), there exists one and only one arrow \(\bar{f}\) making the following diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=3em{ \mathcal{F}(\mu) \ar[r]^-{\bar{f}} \ar[d]_-{\phi} & \mathcal{F}(\lambda) \ar[d]^-{\phi_j} \\ \mathcal{F}(\mu') \ar[r]_-{\psi_j} & \mathcal{F}(\lambda_j) } \] The arrow \(\bar{f}\) does not change when we replace \((f_j)\) by a finer covering given by \(f_k : U_k \longrightarrow U_{j(k)}\), when we replace \(\lambda_j\) with \(\lambda_k\), equipped with \(\phi_k \in \operatorname{Hom}_{f_k}(\lambda_k, \lambda_{j(k)})\), and when we replace \(\phi_j(\psi_j)\) with \(\phi_{j(k)} \circ \phi_k\) (\(\psi_{j(k)} \circ \phi_k\)).
We leave it to the reader to deduce that \(\bar{f}\) only depends on \(f\).

(iii) Let us prove that \(\bar{f}\) is functorial in \(f\). Let then \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{W \ar[r]^-{h} & V \ar[r]^-{g} & U} \] in \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\lambda^\circ, \mu^\circ, \nu^\circ\) in \(\operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_U\), \(\operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_V\) and \(\operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_W\) respectively. There is a diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix@R=1.8em@C=2em{ & \nu^\circ \ar[r] & \mu^\circ \ar[r] & \lambda^\circ \\ \nu^1 \ar[ur] & & & \\ \nu^2 \ar[u] & & \mu_j \ar[uur]_-{f_j^i} & \\ & \nu_{jk} \ar[ul] \ar[uul]^-{e_{jk}^i} \ar[ur] & & } \] such as
b) \(i\) being set, the \(e_{jk}^i\) overlap \(W\) and the \(f_j^i\) overlap \(V\).

We first construct \(\mu^1\), \(\nu^1\) and \(\nu^2\) by (\(\mathcal{L}\)0), and the \(\mu_j\) as in (ii). Let \(W'_{jk}\) be an overlay of \(W\) going into overlay \(V_j = t^*(\mu_j)\) as required for the diagram. Refining \(W'_{jk}\) and applying (\(\mathcal{L}\)0) and (\(\mathcal{L}\)1), we obtain a diagram not necessarily commutative, of the required type, satisfying a) and b). Further refining and applying (\(\mathcal{L}\)2), we make it commutative.

It then remains to contemplate the following commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=2.5em{ \mathcal{F}(\lambda^\circ) \ar[r]^-{\bar{g}} & \mathcal{F}(\mu^\circ) \ar[r]^-{\bar{h}} \ar[d] & \mathcal{F}(\nu^\circ) \ar[d] \\ \mathcal{F}(\mu^1) \ar[r] & \prod \mathcal{F}(\mu_j) \ar[r] & \mathcal{F}(\nu^1) \ar[d] \\ \mathcal{F}(\nu^2) \ar[rr] & & \prod \mathcal{F}(\nu_{jk}) } \]

(iv) It is clear that \(\bar{g}\) is functorial in \(\mathcal{F}\): any sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(\mathcal{A}^*\) is therefore a direct image by \(t\) of a pre-beam \(\mathcal{F}_0\) (only determined) on \(\mathcal{A}\), and any sheaf morphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow \mathcal{G}\) is image of one and one single prebundle morphism \(f_0 : \mathcal{F}_0 \longrightarrow \mathcal{G}_0\).

It already follows that \(t_*\) is fully faithful; it remains to be proven that \(\mathcal{F}_0\) is a beam. If \(f_j : U_j \longrightarrow U\) is an overlap, this overlap is image of an overlap in \(\mathcal{A}^*\), so that \(\mathcal{F}_0(U)\) injects into \(\prod_j \mathcal{F}_0(U_j)\). If \(x_j \in \mathcal{F}_0(U_j)\) stick together, then, a fortiori, they stick together in \(\mathcal{A}^*\) therefore come from a element of \(\mathcal{F}_0(U)\). This completes the proof of (8.1.12).

Let \(q : \mathcal{C} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), \(p : \mathcal{L} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) and \(\mathcal{L}^*\) as in (8.1.10) and \(\mathcal{D}\) a field on \(\mathcal{C}\). The \(q_* \mathcal{D}\) field on \(\mathcal{B}\) is the field “defined” by \((q_* \mathcal{D})_U = \mathcal{D}_{q^* U}\).

If \((X_\lambda)_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}}\) is a local projective system indexed by \(\mathcal{L}\) with value in \(q_* \mathcal{D}\), we define a local projective system indexed by \(\mathcal{L}^*\) and with value in \(\mathcal{D} : (X_L)_{L \in \mathcal{L}^*}\) by the formula \[ X_L = \phi^* X_\lambda \quad \text{pour } L = (V, U, \phi, \lambda) . \] We leave it to the reader to verify that:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.13.1

With the previous notations, we have \[ \varprojlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} X_\lambda \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varprojlim_{L \in \mathcal{L}^*} X_L , \] the two members being simultaneously defined or not.

Let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a site and \(p^* : \mathcal{B}^* \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a category locally filter on \(\mathcal{B}\). If \(\mathcal{C}\) is a field on \(\mathcal{B}\), then \(p_* \mathcal{C}\) is a field on \(\mathcal{B}^*\) (8.1.13.0); moreover, the identical functor: \(\mathcal{B}^* \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}^*\) is a locally filtering category on \(\mathcal{B}^*\), provided with the induced topology, and all local inductive system \((X_\lambda)\) indexed by \(\mathcal{B}^*\) defines a local inductive system, indexed by \(\mathcal{B}^*\), on \(\mathcal{B}^*\).
Easily checked:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.14

With the previous notations, we have \[ \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{B}^*/\mathcal{B}} X_\lambda = \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{B}^*/\mathcal{B}^*} X_\lambda , \] the two members being simultaneously defined or not.
8.2. Local inductive limits in beam categories
Let \(p : T \longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of sites. Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be the field on the following \(S\)
(i) An object of \(\mathcal{C}\) is a pair \((U, \mathcal{F})\) of an object of \(S\) and a beam \(\mathcal{F}\) of \(p^* U\).

(ii) An arrow \(f : (U, \mathcal{F}) \longrightarrow (V, \mathcal{G})\) is a couple formed by an arrow \(f_0 : U \longrightarrow V\) and a morphism of \(T/U\)-sheaves: \[ f_1 : p^*(f_0)^* \mathcal{G} \longrightarrow \mathcal{F} \]
(iii) The functor of \(\mathcal{C}\) in \(S\) is given by \(f \longmapsto f_0\).
We take care that \(\mathcal{C}_U\) is the opposite category of the category of beams on \(p^* U\).
Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a locally co-filtering category (on the left) on \(S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.2.1

With the previous notations, the category of local inductive systems, indexed by \(\mathcal{L}\), of bundles on \(T\), is the category \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{C})^\circ\).

The "projective limit" functor of 8.1.3 is called here "local inductive limit" functor. It's a functor of \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{C})^\circ\) in \((\Gamma\mathcal{C})^\circ = \widetilde{T}\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.2.2

The "local inductive limit" functor of the category of inductive systems of beams on \(T\), indexed by \(\mathcal{L}\), in the category of beams on \(T\), is everywhere defined, commutes at finite projective limits and commutes at arbitrary inductive limits.

Propositions 8.1.13, 8.1.14 and 8.1.12 allow us to return to the case where \(S = T = \mathcal{L}\). A system local inductive is then the data, for each \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S\), of a beam \(\mathcal{F}_U\) on \(S_{/U}\) and, for each arrow \(f : V \longrightarrow U\) in \(S\) of an arrow, functorial in \(f\), of \(f^* \mathcal{F}_U\) in \(\mathcal{F}_V\).
For such inductive systems, the \(\varinjlim\) are calculated as follows:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.3

Let \(U \longmapsto \mathcal{F}_U\) be a section of the pre-bundle category on the objects from \(S\). We have \[ a(U \longmapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_U)) \simeq \varinjlim_U a \mathcal{F}_U \]

By definition, the right hand side represents the functor which, at each sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(S\), associates all coherent systems of arrows \(\phi_U : \mathcal{F}_U \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}|U\). To his tower, an arrow \(\phi_U\) is a coherent system of arrows \(\phi_g\), one for each \(g : V \longrightarrow U\), \(\phi_g : \mathcal{F}_U(V) \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}(V)\). Let's apply 8.1.11 to the identical functor of \(S\), thus obtaining \(S^*\) locally filtering on \(S\). We saw that \[ \varinjlim_{g \in S} \mathcal{F}_U(V_g)^\sim \simeq \varinjlim_U a \mathcal{F}_U , \] where \(V_g\) is the source of \(g\) and \(\sim\) the "generated constant beam" functor. The functor \(U \longmapsto 1_U\) of \(S\) in \(S^*\) is cofinal, hence again by 8.1.5 \[ \varinjlim_U \mathcal{F}_U(U)^\sim \simeq \varinjlim_U a \mathcal{F}_U . \] Returning to the definitions, we finally find \[ \varinjlim_U \mathcal{F}_U(U)^\sim \simeq a (U \longmapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_U)) . \] This shows that in the case to which we have reduced ourselves, the functor \(\varinjlim\) is everywhere defined, and that it is calculates as composed of the functor "beam generated by a pre-beam" and the functor \((\mathcal{F}_U) \longmapsto (U \longmapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_U))\). These two functors commute at finite projective limits, and the functor \(\varinjlim\) commutes moreover to arbitrary inductive limits by its definition as a functor deputy.

Lemma 8.2.3 provides a systematic method for demonstrating properties of the inductive limit functor local from the analogous properties of the “bundle associated with a prebundle” functor. We find thus:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.2.4

Let \(T\), \(S\) and \(\mathcal{L}\) as before, \(\mathcal{A}_\lambda\) be a system local inductive system of rings on \(T\), \(\mathcal{L}_\lambda\) (resp. \(\mathcal{M}_\lambda\)) a local inductive system of module bundles on the right (resp. left) on \(\mathcal{A}_\lambda\), all three indexed by \(\mathcal{L}\). We have \[ \varinjlim_\lambda (\mathcal{L}_\lambda \otimes_{\mathcal{A}_\lambda} \mathcal{M}_\lambda) = \varinjlim_\lambda \mathcal{L}_\lambda \otimes_{\varinjlim_\lambda \mathcal{A}_\lambda} \varinjlim_\lambda \mathcal{M}_\lambda . \]

(Return to the case \(T = S = \mathcal{L}\), and note that the two members coincide with the generated sheaf by the pre-bundle of \[ \mathcal{L}_U(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{A}_U(U)} \mathcal{M}_U(U) ) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.2.5

Let \(T_1 \overset{q}{\longrightarrow} T_2 \longrightarrow S\) be two site morphisms, and \((F_\lambda)\) a local inductive beam system on \(T_2\) indexed by a locally co-filtering category \(\mathcal{L}\) of \(S\). We have \[ q^* \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} F_\lambda \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} q^* F_\lambda . \]
This immediately results from the fact that \(q^*\) admits an assistant on the right \(q_*\).

Let \(S\) and \(T\) be two sites in which finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable, i.e. \(p : T \longrightarrow S\) a morphism of sites such that \(p^* : S \longrightarrow T\) commutes to finite \(\varprojlim\), and let \(\mathcal{L}\) be the locally co-filtering category on \(T\) having as objects the triples \((V, U, \phi)\) where \(V \in \operatorname{Ob} T\), \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S\) and \(\phi \in \operatorname{Hom}(V, p^* U)\). We gets it by applying 8.1.11 to \(1_S : S \longrightarrow S\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.2.6

For any beam \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(T\), we have \[ \mathcal{F} = \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} \phi^* \phi_* \mathcal{F} \] where, by abuse of notation, \(\phi\) designates the morphism induced by \(p\) from \(T/V\) into \(S/U\).

Let \(\phi'\) be the reciprocal image functor in the sense of presheaves; from 8.2.3, we take \[ \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} \phi^* \phi_* \mathcal{F} = \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} a \phi' \phi_* \mathcal{F} = \varinjlim_{\lambda \in \mathcal{L}} \mathcal{F}(V)^\sim = \mathcal{F} . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.2.7

Let \(p : T \longrightarrow S\) be a topos morphism, \(\mathcal{O}_S\) be a sheaf of rings on \(S\) and \(\mathcal{O}_T\) its reciprocal image on \(T\). So that a \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Module on the right \(\mathcal{M}\) is flat on \(\mathcal{O}_T\), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \[ (8.2.7.1) \qquad \mathcal{N} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} p^* \mathcal{N} \] of the category of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-Module on the left in that of abelian beams on \(T\) is exact.
The assertion “one must” is trivial; let us therefore assume the exact functor (8.2.7.1), and prove that \(\mathcal{M}\) is flat.

We can assume \(p\) defined by a morphism of sites \(p : T \longrightarrow S\) verifying the hypotheses do it in 2.6. Let \(V \in \operatorname{Ob} T\), \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S\) and \(\phi : V \longrightarrow p^* U\). We also denote by \(\phi\) the induced site morphism: \(\phi : T/V \longrightarrow S/U\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.8

The functor \(\mathcal{N} \longmapsto \mathcal{M}|V \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_V} \phi^* \mathcal{N}\), of the category of \(\mathcal{O}_S|U\)-modules on \(S/U\) in that of \(\mathcal{O}_T|V\)-modules on \(T/V\), is correct.

We come back to taking \(V = p^* U\). Let \(j\) and \(j'\) be the "inclusion morphisms": \(j : S/U \longrightarrow S\) and \(j' : T/p^* U \longrightarrow T\). We have: \[ j'_!(\mathcal{M}|V \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_V} \phi^* \mathcal{N}) = \mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} p^* j_! \mathcal{N} , \] hence 8.2.8 since \(j_!\) and \(j'_!\) are exact and faithful.

Let \(Q\) be a \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Module. We have (8.2.6) \[ Q = \varinjlim_{\phi : V \to p^* U} \phi^* \phi_* Q|V \] so that (8.2.4) \[ \mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} Q = \varinjlim_{\phi : V \to p^* U} \mathcal{M}|V \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_V} \phi^* \phi_* Q|V \] According to 8.2.8, the functors \(Q \longmapsto \mathcal{M}|V \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_V} \phi^* \phi_* Q|V\) are exact at left, so that \(\mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} Q\) is left-exact at \(Q\), therefore exact.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.2.9

Let \(f : (T, \mathcal{O}_T) \longrightarrow (S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a topos morphism ringed. Then, the reciprocal image by \(f\) of a bundle of flat (right) modules is a bundle of modules dishes.

Factorizing \(f\) by \((T, f^* \mathcal{O}_S)\), we return to the case \(\mathcal{O}_T = f^* \mathcal{O}_S\). Either then \(\mathcal{M}\) a bundle of flat modules on \(S\). According to 8.2.7, to verify that \(f^* \mathcal{M}\) is flat, it is enough to check the correctness of the functor \[ \mathcal{N} \longmapsto f^* \mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} f^* \mathcal{N} = f^*(\mathcal{M} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathcal{N}) \] hence the assertion, since \(f^*\) is correct.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.10

Let \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) be a ringed topos, \(\mathcal{F}\) be a bundle of modules with left locally of finite presentation, \(\mathcal{G}\) a bundle of bimodules and \(\mathcal{H}\) a flat bundle of modules on the left. So, the canonical arrow \[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H} \longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H}) \] is an isomorphism.

The question is local to \(S\), which allows us to assume that \(\mathcal{F}\) admits a finite presentation \[ \mathcal{F}_1 \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}_0 \longrightarrow \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow 0 \] The first line of the following diagram is correct because \(\mathcal{H}\) is flat: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.5em@C=1.6em{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H} \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{S} & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}_2, \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H} \ar[d]^-{S} \\ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathcal{F}_2, \mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H}) } \] hence the assertion. Doing \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{A}\), we deduce from 8.2.10:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.11

Any morphism of a bundle of modules locally of finite presentation in a bundle of flat modules is factorized, locally on \(S\), by a free bundle \(\mathcal{O}_S^n\) (\(n \in \mathbf{N}\)).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.2.12 (D. Lazard)

So that a bundle of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on a ringed site \((S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) is flat, it is necessary and sufficient that it be local inductive limit of free modules of finite type.
The "it is sufficient" results from 8.2.2, 8.2.4.

Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S\), or \(\mathcal{L}_0(U)\) (resp. \(\mathcal{L}_1(U)\)) the category bundles of free modules of finite type (resp. of finite presentation) on \(U\), provided with an application linear in \(\mathcal{M}|U\). For any arrow \(f : V \longrightarrow U\) in \(S\), let \(f^*\) be the functor restriction of \(\mathcal{L}_i(U)\) in \(\mathcal{L}_i(V)\). Functors define a category \(\mathcal{L}_i\), fibered on \(S\), whose fibers are the opposite categories of the categories \(\mathcal{L}_i(U)\).

The reader will check that the category \(\mathcal{L}_1\) is locally filtering on \(S\), and that \[ \mathcal{M} = \varinjlim_{(M, f) \in \mathcal{L}_1} M \] If \(\mathcal{M}\) is flat, the inclusion of \(\mathcal{L}_0\) in \(\mathcal{L}_1\) is fully faithful and, from 8.2.11, checks (C\(\mathcal{F}\)1) from 8.1.4. It then automatically checks (C\(\mathcal{F}\)2), and \(\mathcal{L}_0\) is locally filtering. According to 8.1.5, we have \[ \mathcal{M} = \varinjlim_{(M, f) \in \mathcal{L}_0} M , \] hence the assertion 8.2.12.
